text: I saw it in Brookstone a few days ago and was totally sold on the idea of `` self-setting '' . Well , that 's actually not very true . Pros : Large display with adjustable levels . The blue display also looks a lot better than the usual green or red . The alarms are easy to use . It `` should '' be able to automatically adjust for Daylight Savings Time . Cons : Brookstone is intentionally vague about what they mean by `` self-setting '' . No , it does n't connect to the US atomic clock as some reviewers say . It was merely pre-set in the factory , and sustained by the battery . If you look at their manual -LRB- -LRB- ... -RRB- -RRB- you will understand why : if you `` reset '' the clock , it wo n't reset to the current time . I did n't realize this until I was trying out the features and inadvertently changed the original clock . I thought by `` resetting '' the clock it will adjust back automatically . Nope . This was the feature that I thought was worth the price tag , but now I just feel cheated . The clock does use a battery -LRB- pre-installed -RRB- . So it is not really `` self-setting '' . While it stores the day\/month\/year info , there is no way to show them on the clock screen . And , it 's a lot easier and more tempting to turn of the alarms than snoozing : the button to turn it off is a lot taller than the snooze button . Overall , it 's not a bad one even if it is not really automatic . I just wish they were more straightforward about what it really is .
label: Alarm Clock

text: I saw this radio at Target and thought it looked really cool . I like the old-fashioned styling and the blue display . Unfortunately , I quickly found two major problems with this . As someone stated earlier , the display is too bright-it lights up my room like a full moon and there seems to be no way to turn it down . The second major problem is that the volume dial does not turn smoothly , rather it is notched in such a way that there really is no way to control the volume . On the unit I have , either the volume is down all the way , okay at the first notch , and way too loud on the second notch . I like the nature sounds , but the bird sounds are rather silly because they sound like snippets from a dozen different habitats and is distracting . The flowing water sounds nice , though . Tuning is interesting because the frequencies are in 0.05 increments . So , to go from 90.5 to 90.7 , you have to push the up button four times instead of just once . While this is not an issue to go up one frequency , it 's annoying to go up 5 or 10 . Lastly , the speaker pooped out on the second night . So , the radio is absolutely useless at this point . The radio is nice looking and a little retro , but unfortunately , there are just too many design problems that make it a waste of money and time . It 's going back to Target first thing Saturday morning .

text: Hello , I had this machine since October 2011 and it had this horrible humming to it since I bought it so I never really used it that much . I `` finally '' ran into a post that said to try a better power supply . I swapped out the power supply with a better , regulated power supply -LRB- input 100-240v , output 12v -RRB- and `` finally '' no more buzzing ! The clock works like a charm now . You can find a replacement power supply at your local Radio Shack or computer store . They are not expensive . Thanks so much to Matthew for the power supply tip and another gentleman who suggested to open up the clock and bend a few of the LEDs to the back so the display would not be so bright . Be sure to tighten the display plate when putting the clock back together since it is pressure sensitive . Two very easy fixes and the sound soother now works nicely without any issues !

text: Finding a good travel alarm with a digital display should n't be difficult , but I 've gone through a number of other clocks that were unsatisfactory . One had a design very much like this one , but the switch was flaky ; if you tried to set the alarm , it would irregularly oscillate between displaying the alarm and the time . Another had a folding cover but left buttons exposed in back ; when I arrived at my destination , I often found that the buttons had been depressed in transit and the time had been reset . The Westclox # 47508 travel alarm does n't have these problems , and has worked perfectly so far . As others have noted , the light is dim and remains on only so long as you press the button . The alarm rings for one minute , and you can snooze indefinitely for four-minute intervals -LRB- although I wish the snooze were a bit longer -RRB- . The buttons and switches are simple and intuitive . It runs on a single L1154 button battery . This battery type goes by various names -LRB- alkaline A76 , LR44 ; silver oxide E357 , 10L14 , MS76 , SR44 -RRB- , and is one of the easier button batteries to find in stores . The clock is compact and appears sturdily made .

text: I have been trying to get the right music reproduction setup in my `` mancave '' for some months . My latest iteration involves a pair of `` lightly-used '' Polk T15 speakers , which I gleaned off eBay for half their regular price . However , I did n't have a dedicated amp to power them and the speakers arrived very quickly after I won the bid - the Wednesday right before Memorial Day weekend . So , the Friday before the holiday , I ordered this amp for Saturday delivery . It arrived a little after 11:00 a.m. on Saturday , via FedEx . I had already pre-wired the speakers so all I had to do was hook up the amp to the speakers and plug it in . I have spent the better part of my 3-day , Memorial Day weekend listening to my favorite songs via Windows Media Player on my Dell PC , playing through this amp . The Polk speakers were rated at 89db spl for efficiency , so I was n't 100 % sure if 50 watts RMS per channel would drive them to loud enough levels . This little amp has plenty of power to drive those speakers to quite loud levels , which tells me that this little amp has some headroom in it 's power output ratihg . Let me get into the pros and cons . Pros : 1 -RRB- Great price at just under 100.00 . 2 -RRB- Very clean sound with no distortion even at high levels . 3 -RRB- Auto standby mode when no input signal . 4 -RRB- Low profile amplifier . 5 -RRB- No fan required . Cons : 1 -RRB- Must use input 2 for continuous signal to avoid auto-switching . 2 -RRB- At very low levels of input or very quiet musical passages , amp goes into standby mode . I 've played the gamut of music from Rock , Jazz , Pop , Classical , Opera and Boom-Boom . This amp is solid . I am very satisfied so far and the price is unbeatable . For small to medium-size bookshelf speakers or relatively high-efficiency speakers , this amp might be a near-perfect selection . I 'll post a picture with this review .

text: I bought two of these units for use in a fringe area . One works as advertised and provides me with signal strength sufficient to lock stations from over 90 Km distant -LRB- line of sight -RRB- using a 19 dB Yagi antenna for the UHF combined with a 15 dB antenna for high-band VHF . I replaced a 15 dB antenna amplifier with the Channelmaster unit because of its higher advertised gain -LRB- 23 dB -RRB- . Without any feed-line amplifier I get no usable signals . With the 15 dB amplifier I lock most but not all of the OTA signals in the UHF band but nothing in the VHF high-band . In particular I can not lock the ABC and CBS affiliates that put their HDTV signals into the VHF high-band -LRB- 7.1 and 13.1 -RRB- , and I can not even detect the two VHF feeds from the PBS station -LRB- 9.1 and 9.2 -RRB- that are low-power and intended for local reception . With the higher gain 7777 amplifier I can lock all the signals . Surprisingly I can lock the two PBS feeds in the VHF high-band . These are transmited at 100W ERP yet I get decent lock on both feeds ! The problem I had with the amplifier was that the first unit I received was DOA . Being a long ways away from vendors it is a pain to return for replacement . I noticed the F-connector on the power box was loose so I opened the box and determined that there are cold solder joints on the board . I resoldered these and now get 23VDC on the line , but the amplifier does n't pass any signal . Opening up this box shows was a lousy piece of S & \* t is inside . Surface mount components and sloppy solder joints all over the board . The Chinese manufacturer is evidently not doing any quality control . I kept this unit -LRB- for the spare PS -RRB- and tried again with a different supplier . The second unit works fine as described above . If it works , fine . If not then be prepared to return it for a replacement and hope that you get one that was assembled on a day when the assembly slaves got a hot breakfast .
label: Amplifier

text: I am impressed with this little Nuforce HDP both as a DAC and as a headphone amplifier ; which is high praise from someone who owns a $ 15,000 headphone rig with Stax SR-007 , Sennheiser HE60 and HD800 headphones , a Woo WES amp , Eddie Current ZDT amp , and PS Audio Perfectwave DAC . The HDP seems to offer a lot of performance in a small package , and easily outperforms my original Nuforce Icon amp that I bought May 2008 for my computer desktop . And the beauty is that it is small enough that I can use the HDP 's superior DAC to feed the original Icon when I want to listen to speakers -LRB- both amps are the same size -RRB- . The feature list is quite full , including 24\/96 bit rate USB and 3.5 mm 24\/96 optical inputs , 24\/192 coax input , RCA or 3.5 mm analog input , 1\/4 '' headphone output , and variable output level RCA preamp out . Not many DACs in this price range offer 24 bit\/96 Khz sampling rates , and even my 2x more costly Apogee mini-DAC only does 24\/48 via USB . The HDP using 24\/96 USB input from my Macbook beats all my other $ 100 - $ 500 USB DAC\/amps in the house , and even beats the headphone output of my Apogee mini-DAC with USB . The HDP 's RCA line-out seems almost equivalent to the Apogee 's 1\/8 '' line-out as well , where the Apogee seems to be a little more refined sounding at times -LRB- if using my upgraded Sigma 11 power supply -RRB- . The HDP is very detailed and transparent , with good sense of air and space , and offers a deeper soundstage than the Apogee headphone out . The HDP bass has great extension and impact , with rich mids and sparkly crisp treble . And it 's just a little to the warm side of neutral , so I can still enjoy it with brighter headphones like my HD800 or Head-direct HE-5 . Likewise it is also a good match for my HD600 headphones and my re-cabled Denon D7000 which can sound darker on the wrong amps . Some amplifiers that are good with full-size headphones may not be a good match with very sensitive in ear monitors -LRB- aka earphones or IEM -RRB- , and they might have noise , or hiss that is not otherwise detectable with full-size phones . Well , that is not a problem here . I find the HDP to be an excellent match with all my very sensitive universal and custom molded in ear monitors , including Monster Turbine Pro Gold , Westone 2 , Westone 3 , Westone ES3X , Ultimate Ears 11Pro and JH Audio 13Pro . It does a very good job with all of them , despite their widely varying sound signatures . The UE11Pro can sometimes be bass heavy or have some recessed midrange with the wrong amp ; but neither of these are a problem with the HDP , even though it is not a bass-lite or forward sounding amplifier . How they managed to squeeze such good sound into a price this low is unclear to me , because you basically have to spend $ 700 - $ 900 for a better DAC -LRB- Apogee or Stello -RRB- and $ 700 - $ 1,000 for a better amp -LRB- upgraded Woo WA6 or ALO Amphora -RRB- , and you 'd have to buy both a DAC and Amp to get most of the features of the HDP . -LRB- Except for analog inputs for iPod or other players , where many DAC 's do n't include those -RRB- . I will conclude by mentioning a couple of issues that I have . The HDP sounds very good with a wide variety of headphones , but it might not be optimal with all of them . With my Head-direct HiFiMan HE-5 and Grado HF-2 , if I try to play those headphones at very high volume levels -LRB- closer to max -RRB- then the sound can get a little strident and fatiguing in the highs . I 've heard this same thing with the HE-5 and a $ 700 Grahm Slee Solo SRG II , and that 's probably because the HE-5 are very difficult to drive low impedance headphone and require a lot of current . With the HE-5 headphones this happens at volume levels that are much higher than my Woo WA6 tube amp can hope to achieve , so if I never tried to play the HDP louder than my WA6 I would not have heard this . The Grado HF-2 can be a little boosted in the upper mids and lower treble , and they just seem to respond better to my tube amps to tone them down a notch when trying to play them at very loud levels -LRB- levels that would be damaging or harmful for more than a few minutes -RRB- . At normal to moderately loud levels this is never an issue . Another issue is that the volume pot can have some channel imbalance at the very low end of the volume control when using very sensitive in ear monitors which do n't require turning up the volume knob very much . This is common with most lower price gear when using IEM , but can be alleviated by turning down the computer system volume if you are using the USB input . However , none of these issues would prevent me from highly recommending this product . It makes me eager to think about trying their high-end products at some point .

text: I bought a set of these batteries just for general use - a Game Boy here or a portable cd player there . They work great , especially in the Game Boy . After a couple of weeks of regular use , I decided to recharge the batteries in preparation for a lengthy trip , even though the Game Boy 's power light had n't yet switched from green to red -LRB- indicating approx . 10 % charge remaining in the batteries -RRB- . In regular portable devices , these batteries have faithfully supplied around 15 hours of life - what one would expect out of ordinary alkaline batteries . My dad got a digital camera awhile back and I bought him a set of the Rayovacs with a charger so he could keep taking pictures . The batteries do n't hold up as well in the camera , but like I said before , they 're very comparable to standard alkalines , which would also lose charge quickly in a digital camera . Fortunately , he now has 3 pairs of AA 's , so as long as he keeps them all freshly charged , he should n't have to worry about being stuck without power . I ran a few tests with the Rayovac i-C3 's , and here are some of my results : 2 AA 's in Game Boy Advance : approx . 15 + hours 2 AA 's in portable cd player : approx . 15 + hours 2 AA 's in digital camera -LRB- pictures -RRB- : 30 minutes -LRB- give or take - either way , it 's still plenty of time to recharge a spare set -RRB- 2 AA 's in digital camera -LRB- video -RRB- : approx . 5 minutes 6 AA 's in Sega Game Gear : approx . 5 hours -LRB- a power-hungry machine ! -RRB- Word of advice : if you use these batteries , remove them from any device you do n't plan on using for awhile , as their power seems to be drained proportionally to the amount of energy the device consumes , even when it 's off . For instance , my dad charged a pair of the i-C3 's , put them in his camera -LRB- turned off -RRB- , then put it away . Only 3 or 4 days later , the batteries had no charge . On the other hand , I hardly notice any loss in charge from the batteries in my Game Boy after a week .

text:
This set includes 4 AA cells Made in Japan for Sony by Sanyo , as well as a timed charger , NOT a smart charger as its similar looking Sony variants .

The Sony Power Charger can recharge 4 AAA or AA cells at a time . Unlike the Panasonic BQ-390 which I also used and reviewed , this Sony uses a timer to stop charging . The Panasonic BQ-390 has advanced circuitry inside to monitor charging through temperature , voltage drop , and the cell 's internal resistance , so that you 'll get more precise charging and less damage from overcharging of simpler chargers . Sony also offers a similarly advanced product , but this model is a cheaper timed model . It charges the 2100mAH cells for 8 hours straight , or 800 mAh AAA cells for 7 hours straight . text: I purchased this player at Christmas for $ 99 . I normally buy Sony products because of their past reliability and quality . On paper , this player sounds great : blu-ray player , Internet media streaming and DLNA . Sadly , it only does one of these things great . As a blu-ray player , it 's excellent . Disks load quickly and the picture quality is great . No problems there . But I bought this player to be the media hub in our house connected to the best TV and both the NetFlix steaming and DLNA are unusable . Netflix is HORRIBLE on this player . At times , movies take 3-5 mins to load , then play for 30 seconds and then stops to buffer again for 2-3 mins . Other times , movies play without a problem . Totally unreliable . I have a Tivo Premiere and a Wii that streams the same movies without a problem . All of the are connected to a 35 mbit download Internet connection inbound to the house with this player hardwired to a 1GB physical network . So it 's not the network . The player is crippled to not list . AVI formatted movies . If you want to play an AVI from a USB drive , by default , the player will tell you `` no playable files found . '' But , if you change the file extension of your movie from . AVI to some other video extension like M4V , MP4 , etc. , the player will then display it in the list -LRB- even though it 's not a MP4 file -RRB- so that you can choose it from the menu and play it . It 's unneeded extra work to have to change the file extension to play a movie file . It seems only in the US that they cripple the player to not list . AVI movies . DLNA is a huge FAIL . I 've tried 4 different DLNA media software with this player . The player sees the computer and the file system allowing you to browse to the files . Once you get to your movie folder , it says `` No Playable File Found . '' Unless you let the software transcode the video on the fly , it wo n't work which lowers quality , etc. . The wireless capabilities of this player are tricky to understand . Beware , this player does not come with wireless capabilities out of the box meaning you ca n't just plug it in and it will connect to your wireless network . You need to buy an additional wireless adapter to connect to this player to allow it operate wirelessly . The term they use is `` wireless capable '' not wireless ready . These are known problems with this player . If you search the Internet , you 'll see alot of people having the same struggles . Do your research and find another player if you want reliable NetFlix streaming and\/or DLNA capability . I gave it a 1 star because it 's only good at 1 thing , playing blu-ray disks . If future firmware updates fix the NetFlix and DLNA issues , then it may get more stars . If it was n't past the 30 days , I would easily send this back .
label: Blu Ray Player

However , the remote that comes with this player has the ability to run the volume , power and a couple other things on your TV , so while watching a Blu-Ray disc , I actually have no need for the Dish\/TV remote . text: The bad first : Any and all comparisons here are to my $ 200 Sony BDP-S570 Blu-ray player -LRB- latest model bdp-S580 is $ 134 -RRB- . Disk Load Time : Oppo 39 seconds , Sony 41 seconds : -LRB- Sorry but the Sony wins read on -RRB- Bluray player Main Menu load time : Oppo : 25-30seconds , Sony 5 seconds . The Oppo-93 is actually even more painfully slow at times and seems to go through 2 splash screens and a reboot before you can get to the Main Menu . Also on the Oppo if you set it up to auto play -LRB- a disk -RRB- and there is no disk , it is not smart enough to default to the main menu -LRB- it just sits at the splash screen -RRB- , the Sony loads fast and directly to the Main Menu without a disk . Internet content : For me , Sony hands down slams the Oppo with Amazon , Pandora and a slew of others that Oppo does not have . Yes this could be personal preference and Oppo has Vudu and Blockbuster where the Sony does not . But I have Amazon Prime and Netflix raised their rates so I left them . Without Amazon and Pandora I still must use my Sony bluray Bugs : After the latest firmware update : BDP9X-52-0707 , the player has strange bugs . Disks do not play sometimes and the tray drawer opens automatically after closing . When switching from TV to Blu-Ray on my home theater the Oppo screen turns red and all content is red and washed out as well . Both bugs are fixed by powering the Oppo on and off . Oppo customer service confirmed the red phase bug was common so it is not my receiver , besides the HDMI is direct to TV and there were no problems like this before the firmware upgrade . Hopefully they will come out with a new firmware update soon or I will ask for a warranty replacement . Now for the good if you 've read this far . Test setup : Marantz SR 8002 , Panasonic 54 1080P Plasma , 2 Avatar Blu-Ray disks paused at the same scene on each player - this scene showed vibrant colors , blacks , and sunlight . Results : On the 1st test , the pictures were exactly the same , no difference in any of the fields . I could n't believe this and realized that feeding both HDMI 's to my Marantz with a single HDMI out to the TV gave the exact same picture . Problem Marantz SR 8002 . In comes one of my favorite features of the Oppo-93 . A second HDMI out . This allows you to have Audio go through to your AV receiver and Video direct to your TV When I set the Oppo up this way the picture quality was noticeably more vibrant , and clear . Note : The Sony Bluray going direct to TV was also more vibrant and clear -LRB- same as Oppo -RRB- but I can not run the player this way with only 1 HDMI out . Audio is more subjective but I do think the Home Theater soundstage is better with the Oppo Oppo supports a slew of other disk formats but I do n't have any of these . I bought this primarily as a bluray player but also for Netflix and internet streaming . Note I use a hardwire Ethernet since this is the fastest and best quality streaming . I did not compare wireless speeds between the two . All in all I wish I had returned this within the 30 days for a refund . For $ 500 I am disappointed in the bugs , internet offerings and even in Oppo customer service which should replace my blu-ray or come out with another firmware update . The firmware updates are pretty slow and I believe there has only been one -LRB- possibly 2 -RRB- since I bought this March 4 , 2011 -LRB- it is now Sept 24 , 2011 -RRB- .
label: Blu Ray Player

text: This player has a nice LCD and looks great , you can tell you are watching blu ray in high definition , I usually do n't use computer animation to test picture quality but ca n't help but to admire how `` crisp '' they look . I have a few disc playing DTS-HDMA and it was able to decode or at least grab the downmix from it . I have tested the HDMI picture it works just fine , 1080P compatible . But I have not tested the HDMI audio whether it can pass the downmix or bitstream -LRB- I think not likely -RRB- , there appears to be no options to toggle this . In most cases this is not an audiophile 's player . I noticed it seems to load BDs faster than my standalone BD player , which was Sony 's 2009 model . Although it may seem pointless , one of the reason I bought this player is because it comes with composite A\/V cable output , which is important to send the picture to a car A\/V system , or you go to a hotel with an older TV . It also comes with home and car adapter and a nice fitting carrying case\/bag . You can convert the carrying case to strap behind a headrest in the car ; I think it 's a pretty crude setup , and there seem to be no room to plug in the side connectors with the unit sitting in the bag . A little flap that opens on the side of the case to make these connections would have been nice . The built in speakers is a bit weak , as in not very loud , but still stronger than most laptop speakers by a margin , and it should work fine in a quiet environment . There are 2 headphone jacks which is a nice touch , be prepared to crank the volume control all the way up , the player plays full dynamite range so the dialogue is a bit soft and explosion is still loud , as intended . But I am concerned if the volume might be too soft on an airplane , but then again you are stuck on the plane with your own 10 '' screen and watching blu ray , I figure you are already ahead of most passengers . One might need to shop around for headphones with more sensitivity . I have hooked up the headphone jack via a Y cable to stereo receiver , this is completely analog , the amount of hiss is acceptable unless you press your ears to the tweeter of your main speakers . I have experienced no buzzing . I have also used the A\/V output in my car with good results . A couple of things `` cons '' I noticed , the battery is not replacable , not officially anyway , but it is lithium so it should last nicely . And it says do not charge using the car adapter . There is a charge\/OFF\/ON , so the car adapter can still power the player in ON position indefinitely . In conclusion , this is a pretty nice product and surprisingly from a entry level brand like RCA . Still a hefty price tag for some . Most people who has not seen HD played on a small LCD like this will argue you can not tell between DVD and BD , maybe if you walk 4 feet away , but I can definitely tell the difference . Target seems to have good customer service , but I wo n't be bringing it back unless something goes very wrong soon . EDIT : Aug 18 . I noticed the unit has problem playing the sound of a few Universal movie titles with DTS-HD-MA sound track -LRB- King Kong , Death Race , HellBoy 2 -RRB- , I would only get sound on the left side only on those movies . I went on the website -LRB- -LRB- ... -RRB- -RRB- and found a new firmware which I put on a USB stick . This fixed the problem . The firmware was not there when I first bought the unit . I put in the my latest blu-ray - Avatar -LRB- 2009 -RRB- , the jungle detail looks amazing , especially when the chopper first fly in to drop off the Avatars . I will have to try the included DVD to see the PQ differences . For equipment reference I have a 54 '' Panasonic Plasma for my `` regular '' viewing . The only problem I have now with Quantum of Solace which have 4 vertical thin lines on the screen . The problem seem to only be with this title , I tried other Fox titles and does n't have this problem .
label: Blu Ray Player

text: The Samsung BD-C550 Blu-Ray Disc Player is a fantastic device . If I were to rate it on the Blu-Ray aspect alone , hit would get top marks . However , if you want to connect it to a DLNA compliant network device like the Buffalo TeraStation Live , you 're going to be disappointing . It can stream music and show pictures from the DLNA media server , but videos will come back with an error of `` File Format Not Supported '' . I brought this issue up with Samsung Technical Support because the exact same file plays perfectly off a USB drive connected directly to the player so it clearly can play the file format . Technical Support stated the player will only play media shared from a PC and not from network connected devices . While Samsung touts the player is DLNA compliant with it 's AllShare capabilities , it will only play media from a PC sharing media through software like Windows Media Player or Samsung 's Media Manager Software . Hopefully Samsung will overcome their misleading advertising with AllShare 's capabilities and fix this issue in future firmware updates , but their technical support says as of now it 's `` just not possible . '' Beyond the grandeur of it 's Blu-Ray capabilities , I highly recommend VUDU . If you 've got the internet connection to support the HDX version of a movie , it will blow you away . It gives you the capability to rent movies in full 1080p and the Dolby Digital 5.1 Plus is just amazing . Further , it is a very capable NetFlix player and having Pandora is just icing on the cake . One thing I found slightly annoying is the fact that the main screen always has Rovi TV Listing and Blockbuster app icons . Why Rovi TV Listings is even on a device that has no interaction with TV is beyond me . The application is useless and the interface is terrible . I imagine the Blockbuster app is very similar to the NetFlix app , but since I use NetFlix I would prefer not to even see the big Blockbuster icon on the main screen . Sumsung technical support confirmed these apps were not able to be deleted or hidden from the main screen as they are an advertised feature with the player . Basically contracted advertisements with Rovi and Blockbuster . Unfortunately the consumer is stuck with it . Overall , these devices are becoming a critical home theater component beyond just Blu-Ray Players . With rumors of capabilities to stream Hulu and other media sites , these might even soon replace Home Theater PCs .
label: Blu Ray Player

text: This is my first Blu-ray player . I 've owned it for less than a week . I was going back and forth between buying this Blu-ray player and buying the OPPO 93 . In the end , I just could n't rationalize paying $ 500 for a Blu-ray player with slower disc loading speeds . If you have an old A\/V receiver and $ 500 then OPPO 93 is for you . Otherwise , this is the best player currently on the market . Of course , new players will be released in January at the CES . Speed : Amazon reviewers were n't exaggerating . This player turns on instantly and the menu is loaded within a second . I 've tried out a few Blu-ray discs and they load in about 10 seconds . The DVDs I tried load instantly . Menu : The menu is as easy as it gets . I have only used setup features and everything has worked perfectly so far . I just updated the player 's firmware to 1.70 . I burned the fmr file to a CD-R and loaded it into the player . It instantly recognized it and updated itself . I do n't have a router yet so that 's the way I have to update the firmware . Settings : The only options I 've changed are : I have BD Live turned off and super resolution set to 0 -LRB- When you put in a DVD , press the display button and set this option to 0 -RRB- . Supposedly the latter makes the DVD upconversion better . Remote : It was responsive and it worked . The layout of the buttons feels just fine to me . I 'm planning on buying a Harmony remote to control all my devices anyways . Overall Impression : When it first arrived I was a little shocked by how small the player was . I 'm used to big , bulky DVD players so it 's so nice to have a smaller device . I actually find myself swiping my hand over the player , lol . I thought that was such a gimmicky thing but it does come in handy . Although , my hand has to be almost right on top of the sensor , but it does work . After spending WEEKS looking for a Blu-ray player , I wish I would 've just bought this one from the get go and saved myself a lot of time . Note : I have not used the WI-Fi to stream anything as I do n't care about that . Also , I have not watched any DVDs on the player so I ca n't speak to the upconversion quality . From everything I 've read over the last few weeks it seems that OPPO 93 is better , but Panasonic is good . I 'll update my review as time goes by .

It looked so crisp and the color was great . Over the course of the next day or two we kept trying out little apps and what not on the player . You Tube , Pandora , Netflix , and the others stream with great speed and do n't have any interruption so that you can watch your video without having to get frustrated . But that is also dependent on you internet connection . The wired connection is much more reliable than wireless and will save you a good chuck of cash . As a whole , I give this product a 5\/5 . text: This is my first blu-ray player . Picked it up last November as part of a Black Friday special . I went with this player because of the mainly positive reviews and because I have a samsung LCD HDTV that I love . Almost from the start , the thing would freeze up all the time on non-bluray discs , usually on the FBI warning or the stupid `` All comments and opinions contained in the commentary blah blah blah '' screens . Opening the tray and closing it again would usually work to get it past this point . Usually meaning about 75 % of the time . Of course then you have to wait through it all again . And that 's another point . Maybe this is typical of blu-ray players , I do n't know , but boot\/load time seems to take forever - for both blu-ray and non-bluray discs . As far as picture quality , it works great when it works . Crystal clear , no glitches , it 's quite nice . Regular DVDs look great too - so much so that I did n't feel the need to ` upgrade ' anything I already had in my collection . The internet features seem to work alright - no problem connecting to my wifi and doing some simple youtube searching . I have n't really messed with netflix , pandora , or anything like that yet . The player will occasionally freeze up on the main menu screen , but not nearly as often as when trying to load discs . All in all , I really wish I had returned this player when I had the chance . I can easily see this one being delegated to basement duties with the kids when we get that room set up and getting a better player for our main use . I wo n't feel too horribly bad when\/if the kids break it .
label: Blu Ray Player

text: I mainly wanted to see if I could boost the signal to fix an issue with a down-line splitter . I was unable to get 2 TVs to work with an in-house splitter , although they had worked OK before . This left me with connecting only one TV , and switching it if I wanted to use the other TV -LRB- kitchen and basement -RRB- . I hooked this new splitter up outside my house at the direct point where the cable line comes to the house . This was split by the cable company into a 4-way splitter serving 4 jacks in the house . As noted , I had split the output from one of these jacks to serve 2 other TVs along with the one at the jack . This used to work , but stopped and I could only go directly to one other TV . So this was a direct replacement for the existing outdoor 4 way splitter . I ran an outdoor extension cord to hook up the power supply -LRB- which connects to the splitter via a short co-ax cable you need to supply yourself -RRB- . A green LED lets you know the splitter is powered . I then reconnected the 4 lines that run into the house and tested everything out . I have no problems with the existing TVs or the cable modem . Not sure if picture quality is better or not , but it certainly is no worse . I then tried out the 2 TVs off the splitter and it now works OK , solving my problem . I have 2 analog TVs with DTA boxes and 4 HD TVs with cable boxes , plus the cable modem , and everything is working well . I am going to look into running a cable line into the house so I can put the power supply in there , and also get some sort of plastic box to put the splitter in -LRB- like the telephone wiring -RRB- so it is a little more protected from the elements . If all of your splitting happens inside the house , then you will not have this issue .

text: I purchased the 5350 router because I 'm a new Comcast customer and I did n't want to pay a monthly fee to lease a router\/modem . I found this device on the Comcast approved list . After my cable was installed , I was having problems with the router . My MacBook would connect to the router but the connection was n't very reliable - my connection would frequently drop which was incredibly annoying . My work laptop running Windows 7 was n't working with the router at all -LRB- it would connect , but I would n't be able to receive any data -RRB- . I was ready to return the Zoom 5350 but I googled my problem and I found some solutions . Specifically , I read that Comcast customer support is telling users of this device to turn off the IP flooding detection feature in the firewall settings . This seems to have worked - my Windows laptop is now able to connect and my MacBook connection is more reliable . To make this change go to http:\/\/192.168.0.1\/ , select Firewall from the top menu , uncheck IP flood detection and click apply . This seems to fix the problem , but I just made the change this afternoon . I will update my post later , if the router becomes more or less reliable . I 'm giving the router 4 stars right now , but I 'd say this is closer to 3.5 stars because the aforementioned fix is n't very obvious -LRB- although Comcast should have made this setting known -RRB- . ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Just a follow-up . I have n't had any major problems with the router since I made the changes described above . I still would keep my rating at 4 stars since configuring the router is n't particularly intuitive .

text: When charter cable upped the rental fee to $ 7 a month it became silly not to buy our own modem . I bought this Motorola surfboard based on good reviews , and after 5 months of use it has proved very reliable , offering consistent , reasonably high speeds without needing to be re-plugged more than once . It 's only drawbacks are a few design flaws . Note : Others have described this modem as nearly identical to the Motorola SURFboard SB6120 except more expensive and with a prettier but warmer-running case . Normally this would steer me toward the older model but because some charter users experienced firmware problems that turned their SB6120s into bricks , I went with the newer model just to be safe . The price is still quite reasonable considering that after one year it will have paid for itself in rental fees . Pros : So far we have speeds equal to the modem we used to rent , and occasionally higher . It was easy to set up and only once have I had to unplug the modem to get internet working , although that may have been a problem with the router . It is important to note that unless you are paying big bucks for super-high speed you may not be getting D3 rates anyway . But unless you 're strapped for cash or certain that you will never , ever upgrade , it 's not a bad idea to have the option and plan for the future . Cons : The SB6121 is sometimes warm to the touch . I would not describe it as hot . However , we have it free standing -LRB- not stacked beneath something else -RRB- and in the cool basement , so it is not a problem . The illuminated LED icons are easy to see but the icons themselves are somewhat obscure . It is useful to know that the top icon actually changes color depending on the speed of your connection -LRB- which will also depends upon the computer its connected to -RRB- so if you get an amber light , do n't assume the modem is n't working . There is no reset button on the modem , as some versions of this model seem to advertise . This is not a huge drawback for me . Bottom Line : For steady speeds equal to what we were renting , a reasonable price in the long run , ease of setup , and long periods of internet without interruption , I give this modem 4 stars . For the minor flaws of charing more for a new case that runs on the warm side and being needlessly cryptic with its cute icons I took off one star .
label: Cable Modem

text: Decided to upgrade from DSL to cable modem . Was going to buy a Motorola Surfboard , but read reviews mentioning reliability issues and compatibility issues with my ISP -LRB- Comcast -RRB- . Reading the specifications the modem would seem to be massively over-engineered for home use and a two year warranty was exceptional among its competitors . DOCSIS 3.0 was a must-have for my purchase . Installation was a snap . Customization for security was easy and I now have the fastest connection I 've ever had and WPS security . Using basic , bargain-level Comcast DSL I get download speeds of 2.5 Mbytes\/sec and uploads around 500K . Wireless signal is very strong and without interference in a building with -LRB- literally -RRB- a hundred competing networks in range . I ca n't slow this thing down no matter how many Macs , PCs , iPods , and Droids I have streaming high-resolution video at the same time . Netflix movies are instantaneous . I have had no compatibility issues with any of my wifi-enabled devices . The modem supports 253 devices at a time , so would be ideal if I were to set up a boiler-room stock operation or technical-support call center in my den ! The device is aesthetically appealing and connecting directly by wire to any of the 4 ports -LRB- have n't tried the USB port yet -RRB- gives a bit more speed . Frankly , I can not find anything that I have to wait to stream or download , so I have n't had cause to do so other than to test . The build , while plastic , feels very solid . There is no external power converter , so the plug does n't monopolize my power-strip . I 've never felt much about modems before , but this one actually gives me pleasure to own . I think this is the perfect N-wireless cable modem for anyone who values speed . It would probably be great for gamers and people using videoconferencing . It will last , has a better warranty than competitors , is at the bleeding-edge of technology , and will support the coming Ipv6 protocol . I realized I was taking a chance when I bought from a maker I did n't know . I 'm glad I did .

text: The Canon Elura 100 is my first venture into MiniDV camcorders . I had previously used Hi8 . Compared to Hi8 equipment , the Elura 100 is an absolute gem . It is amazingly compact and easily fits into one hand . My hands are relatively large and the controls are relatively small . I was concerned that I might find it difficult to operate . Happy surprise : all the controls come naturally to hand -LRB- or more literally , finger -RRB- and are easy to operate without error . The joystick control , however , is a mixed blessing for controlling playback . It is far too easy to slip out of playback mode . Not a big deal , but an occasional irritant . Slipping out of playback mode simply means you might have to rewind or fast forward to get to the point you want . Video quality in daylight situations is excellent . For the casual user , the Elura 100 will be more than adequate , though comparison with video from a 3ccd camera will show significant difference . Considering that 3ccd cameras are available for less than $ 200 more , potential buyers who feel passionate about video quality might want to carefully compare the Elura 100 with 3ccd models and the capacity of their wallets . 3ccd models seem to run a little larger than the Elura 100 which may also be a consideration for travelers and those who prize low bulk . Handholding the Elura 100 , with the optical stabilization enabled , produces excellent results . Battery life is adequate , though I would suggest investing in one or two extra batteries just to be on the safe side . There are some negatives to the Canon Elura 100 . Low-light performance is poor , which should not be unexpected . Videos made in low-light - and by that I mean subdued indoor lighting - are very noisy -LRB- grainy -RRB- . Because the Elura has no accessory shoe , mounting an auxillary light source will require you to use a third-party handle with an accessory shoe which detracts from the convenience of the compact form factor . The built-in LED intended to provide illumination is a bit of a joke . It is too weak to make a substantial difference and its overly blue hue adds an unfortunate cast to your video . Motor hum can also be bothersome under some circumstances . The automatic gain control is , I think , a bit too aggressive . When recording ordinary conversation , the auto gain is turned high , capturing the motor noise . In my unit it was n't horrible , but it was noticeable . Another reviewer has noted that Canon suggests introducing `` white noise '' such as a radio or television in the background . That is n't really white noise , it 's just plain old noise that will compete with the audio you actually want . The Elura does have an external microphone jack . An external directional microphone is always a better idea , but once again the lack of an accessory shoe creates a problem . For those shooting scenics and outdoor or even most indoor activities -LRB- i.e. , parties -RRB- the motor noise wo n't make much of a difference . For those who are truly finicky or recording low voices without background noise , the hum may drive one up a wall . Overall , the Canon Elura 100 has more in the plus column than in the minus . It 's compact size and overall high performance make it a keeper . But for those who really want the highest possible quality in video and audio without going to truly professional equipment , they may choose to spend more and get a 3ccd camcorder . A couple of other neutral points worth mentioning . The Elura has a still photo capability which is not particularly useful , but might come in handy under some unusual circumstance . The 16:9 aspect ratio is nice for those who like it . Jerry
label: Camcorder

text: This camera is an important upgrade in image quality and features . I replaced a Sony HDR-CX100 by this camcorder and the upgrade is noticeable . I guess this is because the new backlight XMOR sensor and of course the Sony G lens . Third , the stabilizer effect is something that I have never seen in any other camcorders : you can walk filming without any undesirable shake or vibration in the filming movie . Also if you film in a car or in a sportboat the steady shot feature in this camera is simple incredible . This helps you a lot not just for filming but also to take photos using the zoom in low light conditions . Fifth , this camcorder power up in a very short time and open and close the lens protector automatically , so you ca n't miss any good shots opportunities . text: Call me a little old-fashioned but why has Sony gone backwards with their camcorders ? I used to have a viewfinder . I used to be able to take videos in total darkness with infrared nightshot . Your telling me Sony does n't have the technology to keep the good features and still give us a tiny palm-sized camcorder ? I was so excited when I first got this out of the box . It was n't until I had to interact with it further than hitting record that I found out this cute little camcorder had some very annoying features . You have to switch from movie mode to the digital photo mode to take a photo -LRB- used to take a photo instantly with just a touch of the shutter button on my old handycam -RRB- Yes I expect the photos to look bad , most photos taken on camcorders do , but the quality of these photos are so bad to the point that I do n't even know why they included this feature ! I could take better photos on my 2mp cell phone ! Deleting video or photos is a real pain . Like a four step process . I can understand when memories are at stake you can be none to careful , but come-on ! I do n't like that the view screen must be open to attach the USB cable . I set my handycam on the floor to reduce the likelihood of accidental just-knocked-my-hundred-dollar-camcorder-on-the-floor-and-broke-my-touchscreen-clean-off incidents . Touchscreen functions are so very small . I have to use a fingernail to access the menus . If you have big beefy fingers do n't buy this camcorder . There are a few good things I would like to say about it though , so not all bad . This camcorder is adorable ! It could fit in a pocket ! Video quality is what I expect or better with a standard definition camcorder . The zoom is awesome , with crisp clarity and a decently steady shot . Indoors it is n't all that bad , somewhat grainy , but not horribly noticeable . Outdoors good , though this is when I mention again that it would n't hurt to have a small viewfinder so you can shoot without so much sun glaring off the screen ! So much of it 's functionality depends on you being able to see that screen ! Outdoors at night its almost useless -LRB- again , Sony USED to have nightshot camcorders . Am I the only weirdo that used this feature ? -RRB- Instead it has a weeny LED that lights up ONLY dead center and three feet away . Strapping a dollar store flashlight to it would provide better range ! Anyway , I said my peace , now to the basement to dust off old relics of the past that suddenly seem to me FAR COOLER than what we make in this future I so looked forward to . Paint me unimpressed .

text: I 've had this camera for three months now and highly recommend it . The HD video quality -LRB- I only use the FX 24 Mbps mode -RRB- is stunning . It is possible to manually adjust these settings but there is only a small multipurpose rotary knob for this purpose . Also , you ca n't simultaneously adjust both aperture and shutter speed . A larger lens would be nice , native progressive mode framing , and XLR microphone inputs . In order to get these features , you would have to spend a few thousand dollars more . I do have a quibble about the `` 12 Megapixel '' still photos this camera is supposed to be able to shoot . Something is missing in them . The stills even lack the depth and vibrancy of the HD video the same camera produces . My wife 's 3.3 megapixel camera -LRB- a Sony -RRB- produces much better stills . text: I have last year 's model of this camera . The entire line of these Digital8 cameras is great for the average family or amateur videographer . There are many plusses to these cameras . text: I 've had the Sony DCR-TRV-510 for about three months now . It 's been a lot of fun to use and learn . Straight out of the box , charge up the battery , slip in a tape and you 're ready to record . With a little practice and skimming of the manual , you can create some perfectly acceptable and fun still -LRB- digital -RRB- pictures . On the video front , the camera is exceptionally easy to learn and use . It takes standard 8mm video tapes - cheaper and more readily available than mini-DV tapes . Zoom lens is excellent although once you pass the optical zoom range and go into the digital zoom , you will likely notice the `` pixilation '' -LRB- you can turn the digital zoom off . -RRB- Tapes can be viewed on the flip out screen - excellent image . One feature I especially like is the one that lets you find the end of the last video recording -- no matter where you stop on a tape , hit this button and it takes you to where you stopped recording . Low light capability is outstanding even without using the Night Light feature . Still pictures are fun . Easy to do once you 've mastered the technique . The picture is only 640 x 480 so it is really only suitable for web stuff or 2 x 3 or 4 x 3 snap shots . text: REVIEW REVISED 6\/8\/10 I 've been using the Vixia HF R100 camcorder for a few days now and the camera itself works fine . But I 'm giving it two stars -LRB- up from one in the first version of this review -RRB- as a way to ding Canon for leaving out software that would make it more practical to transfer and use video files on a Macintosh . The camcorder comes with software from Pixela that lets you transfer files from the camera to a Windows PC , edit your videos , and export to common formats like WMV or MPEG-2 . But it does n't include a Mac version of this software -- if you want that , you have to go to Pixela and buy it separately for $ 100 . I do n't see why Mac owners should be penalized , and there 's no warning in the product description that the software supplied is Windows-only . If I 'd done more homework before my purchase I would have learned that the Vixia HF R100 records in a newish high-definition format called AVCHD and outputs a file format , `` mts , '' that ca n't be viewed or edited directly on a Mac . You have to import and convert your . mts files using Pixela , Roxio 's Toast , or an editing program like iMovie or Final Cut Pro before you can work with them . None of this is explained in the advertising for the Vixia or even in the instruction manuals . My whole experience with the software side of the Vixia makes me ache to be back in the land of Apple products , where everything just works .

text: I was n't going to write a review , but after re-reading some of the prior reviews and one of them giving 1 star for a poor user interface , I feel compelled to offset the reviewer 's 1 star with my 5 stars . I 've had the cx110 for over a week and love it and see no reason for the prior reviewer 's 1 star rating . The cx110 is small and light weight and comes with us to all family events . It is is a huge improvement over my old Hi8 camera purchased 8 years ago . Now , I do n't feel like a dork pulling out a brick of an antiquated video camera to preserve memories . I found a great deal on a 8gb SDHC class 6 card at frys dot com via slickdeals dot com . I 'm recording at the 2nd highest level quality -LRB- FH -RRB- and the 8gb provides ~ 50-60 mins of recording time . The highest quality setting provides ~ 40-50 mins of recording time . The quality of recorded video\/audio is excellent for the size and price of the camera . It handles low light pretty well in my opinion . Natural light colors come through great . My plan is to take the . mts files and burn them to a DVD and play them back on my samsung c5500 blue ray player . I guess this has something to with AVCHD or something which I 'm still trying to figure out . The bad thing now , is my older Dell struggles to playback the HD video . The camera 's provided PMB software looks okay , as it imports the mts files and will categorize them by date\/time into a calendar view which is nice . It also includes a trimmer\/editor in which you can cut out the beginning and\/or end of a video clip and save it as a new file . The PMB program will also take the files you select and make a dvd out of it . I know there are other editing\/authoring programs out there , but this one is free and included with the camera . Note : if your computer has choppy\/stuttering play back issues like mine , check out a free player called Splash -LRB- lite -RRB- . Play back is a lot is smoother but still not the best -LRB- which I attribute to my specs : older P4 dual core and older 256mb Ati video card -RRB- . Supplied with the cx110 is a usb cable , composite cable for SD tv viewing and component cable for HD tv viewing . I bought a mini HDMI cable on amazon for ~ $ 5 , and have yet to use it . I also bought a 30mm UV filter to protect the lens for ~ $ 6 on amazon as well ... Small investment to protect a $ 400 + camera . In summary , if you 're thinking about this camera go for it . I 'm glad I pulled the trigger on this camera , as our baby coming in Dec 2010 , will have plenty of HD memories to look back on when he\/she gets older . Not to mention my current sons 7 and 4 yrs , love watching videos of themselves , and the cx110 is perfect as we can watch videos instantly , as many times as we want . And also delete unneeded videos when ever we want . DO IT ! Send me a comment if you have any questions .

text: First off , let me clarify that I am a still photographer , not a videographer . That having been said , I purchased this camcorder for its price , the HD capability -LRB- LOL -RRB- and the fact that it was easy to use . The camera hit the mark on 2 of these 3 , but failed miserably on the other . Saying that this camcorder is HD is like saying the express stagecoach is high speed transportaiton in the day of 200 + mph trains & planes . I returned the unit the day after I purchased it having played around with the camcorder to familiarize myself with it and recording 1 event . I recorded the event in 1080P mode , the best quality that the camera has to offer . The event was my daughter and a friend making a presentation with a slide show on the screen to their left . I set up so that the wide angle would get the girls and the screen and the zoom would pretty much fill the screen with the girls . -LRB- NOTE : The zoom on this unit is very limited , but you should know this from playing with it in the store pre-purchase , so this is not a factor in the negative review . -RRB- I tried to give this a 0 star rating , but Amazon only goes as low as 1 star . When I transfered the files to my computer , I was unimpressed with the wide angle picture quality and when the unit zoomed in on the girls , you could not even recognize them due to all the noise in the video . The sound quality of the unit was pretty good , but the picture was apallingly bad . Figuring that this must improve if I plug it into the HDTV in the living room , I did and the results were equally bad . When I took the camcorder back , the vendor has a policy of 15 % to restock . I showed them the video on one of their computers and they agreed that it was not as represented by the manufacturer and they waived the restocking fee . I must say , I had no problems with the buttons or the build quality of the unit that others have noted , but the purpose of any camcorder is to provide quality video . When the manufacturer goes out of its way to indicate that the unit has `` HD '' quality , one expects the results to live up to that claim . I actually get better video from my even less expensive , entry level Coolpix still cameara that I leave in my briefcase for those times you just need a camera and do n't have the good stuff with you . Conceptually , having a small video\/still camera handy is a great idea - if it can deliver on the image quality . I am not sure if others have made it work in the small form factor camera , but JVC needs to go back to the drawing board .

text: I 'm a serious amateur who has used the D70s for nearly three years for paid gigs and personal use . Focusing and frame rate are fast enough for nearly every event I 've ever been to . Photojournalists will be pleased , but sport shooters may want to look for another option . do n't let the lower MP count scare you away from this little gem ; prints enlarge just fine up to 11X14 -LRB- I do n't usually print larger than that -RRB- . Battery life on the D70s leaves nothing to be wanted . I 've shot almost all day on one charge . if you bring a spare battery , you 'll have no worries . The LCD is on the smaller side , but its adequate for checking exposure , and you can also detect any camera shake . No complaints . The menu system is very straightforward and easy to use . Controls on the camera body are placed within easy reach and you can change most settings without removing your eye from the viewfinder -- a big plus . Metering is usually very good , but sometimes I 've had to use compensation to nail exposure . some people make this out to be a huge problem , but it really is n't . just know enough about your camera to make it do what you want it to do under any lighting conditions . I gave the D70s a four instead of five because after a couple years of use , the built-in flash seems to be dying an early death . It does n't fire all the time anymore . Not a huge problem if you use speedlights attached to the body , but it has become a problem in the nikon creative lighting system , which is one of the reasons I wanted the camera . All in all , a wonderful camera to use . its well-built , allows for full creative control , keeps up at most events and allows for full use of nikon lenses .

text: ... I must say I really love this camera because of its great features and modest price . Initially I gave this review 3 stars mainly because of the reliability issues -LRB- or maybe just my bad luck -RRB- but I should note that I am very impressed by the support I 've received from Kodak so I 've raised it to four stars . Initially I noticed a tendency , once in a while , for the camera to freeze while you 've been videoing something for a long time . I have noticed several other reviewers complaining of the same thing . When that occurs , the only remedy is to remove the battery as even the on\/off button fails to work . The file it was creating is , of course , toast , but you can mitigate this problem to some extent by taking shorter clips . When running for a long time , particularly in the sun , it can get pretty hot and I suspect that might be at the root of that problem . The first couple of months I had it , I used it a many times to take videos of my grandchildren in the pool and at the beach . The camera caused a sensation , with a lot of people assuming I was swimming with my cell phone ! The video quality does suffer from instability issues in those clips . I find the electronic video stabilization does n't work very well and I usually just leave it off , but what do you expect for a camera of that price ? If you want to take nice stable videos , use a tripod ! If you do and if you have reasonably good lighting you can take some really stunning full 1080p HD videos in or out of the water ! After a couple of months or pretty heavy use , the camera suddenly would not take a charge or make a logical USB connection to my computer . I called , and Kodak agreed it was broken . I sent it back to them and in a few weeks it was returned working like new , but with the original freeze problem still happening occasionally . The repair shop said they replaced all of the electronics and adjusted it to specs . I was generally pleased with their work . I continued to use it frequently , but did not use it under water until very recently -LRB- about two months after it was repaired -RRB- . When I got home and charged the camera I noticed some condensation forming on the inside of the video display and on the lens . I called , and Kodak agreed to take it back yet again . Before I sent it in I was able to dry it out in the sun with the doors open and decided to use it one more time last weekend before sending it back . Guess what ? The original USB connection problem came back again ! This time they told me they would replace the camera . It 's unfortunate the USB problem occurred again , but it is very good news that Kodak is really standing behind their product warranty which , I should note , is very generous one year with full parts and labor ! I just received what looks like a brand new camera . I certainly hope Kodak gets the kinks out of it because it really is one of my favorite toys .

text: I love this camera - that 's the bottom line for me . I 'm taking a big step up from my first digital camera , the Nikon D40 which my husband wanted so he could start digital photography , and I could not be happier with my choice . However , I think it would be a waste of money for any one not familiar with the adjustments we used to routinely make with 35mm SLR film cameras . The D7000 can certainly function as a fine point and shoot . But why spend almost twice as much on this over the lighter D3100 if you just want to point and shoot , and maybe edit with PhotoShop or some such ? For me , it 's worth the difference because I want the freedom to quickly and easily make adjustments such as setting aperature or shutter speed to freeze or blur action ; adjust the white balance on the fly for what I want the image to be ; adjust the autofocus field and metering to suit the particular situation ; switch from single shot to continuous , etc. . The D7000 puts all that in menus and sub-menus like the inexpensive Nikons , but it also puts it out right on the camera body , available through just one button and the turn of a wheel . Also , this ended up being just about as inexpensive for me as the D3100 because I would not have been satisfied with the kit lens on the D3100 - and replacing it would have been more expensive than using all the wonderful Nikon lenses I have left from the film days - WHICH NOW ALL WORK BEAUTIFULLY ON THE D7000 . -LRB- Of course , I did get my D7000 for $ 1,199 , not the current prices . -RRB- The inexpensive Nikons keep down weight and cost by not including a focusing motor in the camera - you have to rely on one in the lens . The D7000 , like other top of the line Nikons , includes its own focus motor . Nice little bonus with the D7000 is its treatment of my old A1 lenses . I have some optically very good , and very fast , A1s . With the D7000 , I can program it to recognize them - with just the push of a button and turn of the wheel again . All I had to do was assign a lens number to each of the lenses and provide the focal length and maximum aperture . Now I can use them with no trouble , I just have to focus them as always . And the switch to go from autofocus to manual focus on the old lenses , or an autofocus lens that ca n't effectively autofocus in the particular situation -LRB- like my autofocus trying to pick up a tiny hummingbird in the middle of a rose bush this morning -RRB- is conveniently located by the side of the lens where my left hand would be going any way to grab the focus ring for manual focus . This is one really sweet camera . It will do whatever you want . The controls are easy and simple to understand if you have the background in photography - or want to learn . As I said , I think people are wasting their money if they buy this just because it is the new , hot camera when it is really more than they need . But , if you want to be able to control the camera settings , and do it quickly without having to mess around with the camera , this is great . NOTE , after reading other reviews . Nikon DSLRs have never had great video , and this does n't seem to be an exception - if you want a video camera , get something else . As for complaint about the manual , I think it goes back to my comments about needing to have some SLR experience or being willing to learn . I found the manual completely understandable - it generally even explained each function a couple of times if you read all the way through the several hundred pages of manual . Best way I 've ever found to deal with this is sit down with the manual and the camera and really work my way through every setting , button , and dial so I know what I have .

text: I 've gotten this camera to replace a Canon S45 that I bought in January 2003 and this review is meant as a contrast to that type of camera -LRB- i.e. , this review may not be too useful if you 're coming to this camera from another point of view -RRB- . I chose the S45 at the time over the top ELPH model back then -LRB- S330 , I think -RRB- because I expected that it would be my only camera for a while and so wanted manual control , RAW support , etc. . As it happens , the Digital Rebel came out in September that year and when I got one , the role of my S45 changed : A take-anywhere camera for taking snapshots and video clips . The latter came a bit as a surprise , because I thought I 'd prefer using my more full-featured miniDV-based video camera . However , it turns out that `` compact '' often beats `` full-featured '' -LRB- the S45 is not as small as the SD800 , but it 's still pretty small compared to my video camera -RRB- . In its new role , the weaknesses of the S45 were -LRB- a -RRB- not compact enough , -LRB- b -RRB- poor low-light performance , -LRB- c -RRB- limited , low-quality movie recording functionality , -LRB- d -RRB- relative slow response times . -LRB- That 's all relative , of course : At the time , it was a reasonably good compromise in those areas . -RRB- On the plus side , the S45 is sturdy , I really like its user interface , and I got some really nice pictures out of it . How does the SD800 stack up in that context ? Very well , but not perfectly . I like its build quality and the subcompact size -LRB- though it 's a bit on the large end of its class ; not a bad thing per se -RRB- . The plastic parts are no worse than the similar parts on the S45 , but I get slightly anxious at the thought of scratching the shiny surfaces . The 2.5 '' screen make for bright and sharp previews -LRB- it feels more fragile than that of the S45 , however -RRB- . Image quality is a step above that of the S45 in general , and easily two steps above for low-light work -LRB- where the image stabilization function and better ISO 400 performance really shine -RRB- . ISO800 seems somewhat usable , beyond my expectation . I do n't care much about some of the more exotic features like `` swap colors '' , but one feature I 've unexpectedly come to appreciate is the face-recognition contribution to focus and exposure : It works , and it works really well ! -LRB- It also ties into a built-in categorization feature , in that the label `` People '' will automatically be assigned to pictures containing faces ; that 's clever too , but I do n't think I 'll use it . -RRB- Unsurprisingly , the wider-angle lens is a boon to many snapshot situations . -LRB- It was one of the factors that prompted me to order the SD800 . -RRB- There is considerable corner softness at the wider end , but for this type of camera it does n't bother me too much . I wish the long end were a little longer of course -LRB- and slightly less compact models from Panasonic do better in this regard -RRB- , but again I do n't feel strongly about that considering the camera 's size . This also appears to be one the most responsive subcompacts out there : It 's certainly a whole lot faster at starting up and zooming than the S45 -LRB- though it still feels sluggish compared to a DSLR -RRB- . The movie clip recording mode is far better than that of the S45 : The higher -LRB- VGA -RRB- resolution and the image stabilization are the main factors there . Still , I wish it could record a using a tighter codec -LRB- like H. 264 -RRB- and I wish the optical zoom could be used during shooting . A 1GB SD card will hold a little over 8 minutes of 640x480 30fps video , but once downloaded I can convert that to a H. 264 file of about 70MB . The digital zoom in movie mode works well in good light -LRB- almost 5X for 640x480 clips -RRB- , but in low light zooming in more than 3x or so results in exceptionally pixel-noisy clips . Finally , the overall interface is relatively intuitive and generally well executed . My one beef is that `` review '' mode is part of the general mode dial . On the S45 -LRB- and Canon 's DSLRs -RRB- , `` review '' is entered using a separate switch -LRB- or button -RRB- , which allows the general dial to indicate the recording mode : A half-press on the shutter and the immediately goes back to recording mode . On the SD800 , if I 'm reviewing images and suddenly see an opportunity -LRB- which happens all the time -RRB- , I have to first fumble with the dial -LRB- being careful to select the right mode -RRB- and only then is the camera ready to shoot . On the plus side , when shooting a picture the camera automatically displays the image shot for a customizable amount of time -LRB- I set it to `` until shutter pressed '' -RRB- and allows zooming into that picture .
label: Camera

text:
I 've owned a Canon EOS Elan IIe for over two years now . In general I 've been pleased with it , but there 's definitely room for improvement . Things I like : the body is lightweight , the controls are easy to use , and there are some great automatic settings that make it easy to take good shots in a variety of conditions -LRB- action shots , scenery , portraits , etc. -RRB- . Also nothing has broken or malfunctioned , which is saying a lot because my camera has been through consistently rough treatment - it gets thrown in a backpack and knocked around quite a bit .

The primary thing that gives me trouble is focusing . The auto-focus usually works OK - the view has three small boxes in it , and the camera senses which box you 're looking at and automatically focuses on whatever is in that box . In most cases , that 's great . But sometimes the auto-focus screws up . When I take a picture of my dog , for instance , it will focus on the tip of his nose instead of his eyes . So sometimes I override the auto-focus , but the manual focus is difficult to use because there 's no split-circle indicator to show you when something is in perfect focus .

Other than that , I 've been very pleased with the EOS , and I 'd recommend it to someone who wants a powerful , easy-to-use camera that gives you a lot of flexibility .
label: Camera

text: The Canon S90 is so handy that my WIFE loves it -- With image quality so good that a SERIOUS photographer loves it too . The Canon S90 `` just works . '' It 's so small , light , and easy to use , that I always have it with me and always get great image quality . -LRB- You do n't get great pictures if the camera 's too bothersome to bring along . -RRB- Panasonic 's LX3 is the one other compact that matches S90 image quality -- But the LX3 's a little too big for my pockets and its low light images are noisier . What the LX3 DOES offer are much wider angle images -LRB- 24mm -- 18mm with adapter lens , vs. the S90 's 28mm -RRB- . For wide and ultra-wide angle shooting , I use the LX3 -- For everything else -LRB- convenience , and low light -RRB- I use the S90 . Both cameras are excellent . text:
Okay , lets look at the camera it fits in the palm of your hand , literally if you are right handed that is . I am , so no problem there . The 2.5 LCD touchscreen folds out on the left side and is used to control various settings , exposure , effects , tape control etc. as well as provide an alternative to the color viewfinder . The LCD can also flip twist and sit back in it s dock facing outwards so you can still access the controls without it sticking out from the camera .

The power connector is on the back as is the battery , record button and function selector -LRB- vcr , off , camera , memory -RRB- that you use your thumb to operate .

On top there is the photo button and zoom control , halfway toward the front there is the nightshot switch , just in front of that the smart shoe .

On the right side , under your hand , are the : memory stick ; tape compartment -LRB- bottom loaded -RRB- ; iLink , USB and AV ports .

On the front of the camera there s the lens , stereo mic and the S-Video port .

The camera comes with a battery , power supply -LRB- charge the battery on the camera -RRB- , USB cable , AV cable -LRB- 3 x phono to headphone style AV jack -RRB- , lens cover , 8MB memory stick , remote control , manual and software on CDROM .

My last experience of video cameras was the old VHS-C system . Editing tapes using a VCR and a cheap mixing deck was no fun and the results were pretty poor .

Digital video technology is another kettle of fish entirely ! This is actually the first camcorder I have owned and so far I am very impressed . The quality is great , the camera is easy to use and I have no problem downloading the footage to my PC or Mac .

The lack of a iLink cable was a pain but I had to buy a firewire card for my PC anyway , and that came with a cable and software for only $ 25 . You can buy the cables online for about $ 10 - do n't pay $ 40 in a store it is not worth it .

The webcam feature is a nice touch , though it only works in Windows . It looks like I can use this feature to stream video to my relatives overseas too , instead of switching to camera I just switch to VCR mode and hit play .

The difference between this and the TRV19 is the addition of S-Video In - the TRV19 is not capable of converting your analog video to digital .

The TRV-33 gives a better still picture , the TRV22 is 640x480 , but since I have a digital still camera anyway I thought I may as well save $ 150 .

The night shot is basically a kind of infra-red mode , LED 's on the front provide illumination and as long as the subject is not too far away -LRB- -RRB- 5ft -RRB- you can film in complete darkness .

There is some motor noise on the soundtrack , which is understandable given the size of the camera and the proximity of the motor to the mic , but it is hardly noticable .

The software is not great . iMovie or Windows Movie Maker are more user friendly . But it does get the job done .

I have only had the camera a week , so these are mostly first impressions , but suffice it to say I m extremely happy with my purchase and I m thoroughly enjoying DV editing on a PC . I have yet to put anything back to tape , but I created a VCD with the supplied software and am happy with the result . Now I just need to get myself a DVD burner
