text: When I needed to replace my old steam iron I came to Amazon and discovered this one sitting at the top of their best seller list . I 'd already been looking at steam irons locally , and was stunned to see some priced close to $ 100 . I do n't do enough ironing to justify that expense , so decided to try this Panasonic model Amazon was selling for a mere $ 18.95 at the time . It arrived quickly and I put it to use right away . Loading the tank with water was easy enough , and the first thing I noticed is how FAST it achieved full temperature . It is very useful to have a low and high steam setting . The curved sole plate sounded like a gimmick to me , but it really is n't . This subtle feature REALLY DOES inhibit creases where they 're not wanted . I never use the spray feature on any steam iron , but I tested it and it worked fine . The iron is neither too light nor too heavy , and it comes with a generous length cord . It is much narrower than my old iron , which makes ironing in tight spots much easier . For me one of the best features of all is the retractable cord - what 's not to like about that ? The cord is conveniently out of the way for storage , and the cord reel serves as a substantial base that keeps the iron from tipping over when it 's sitting upright . Nothing will ever make me love ironing , but I 'm very happy with the performance of this steam iron ... add my five-star recommendation to the others .
label: Iron

text: This is an average iron . It has a few more steam functions than most basic models , but it also is a bit lacking in other areas . The extra steam functions are nice . It steams upright and horizontal . I do appreciate that aspect of the iron . You can set the steam for high , moderate , or off . Most irons you either have steam or you do n't . It is too light . I like a very heavy iron . I like the weight of the iron to help with the ironing , not just rely on the steam feature . It is also an awkward size and shape for me . It is very long and big , though the weight is n't reflected in the size . When it is filled with water , the weight is better for me , but still no where near ideal for me . With sewing , I like an iron that properly presses , which needs to be heavier than this iron is . It does glide well , but I find that the shape makes it difficult to properly iron small things . I was pretty excited about the steam for ironing my sons ' shirts -LRB- 6 months , 2 years , and 4 years -RRB- because I do n't like using starch on their clothing . But it is so difficult to maneuver this large iron on small clothing . I managed , but not to my liking . I do like that the water compartment completely closes to avoid spilling the water . However , I have had leak issues with the iron . The controls on the iron are in a very awkward location . It is difficult to see the settings and change the settings . The dial is up under the handle , which just makes it awkward to get to . All in all , this is not a bad iron , it is simply mediocre . It is n't good for sewing and is only average for the rest of my ironing needs . As a plus , it did come with a water container to pour water into the iron . I like that .
label: Iron

text: This is an almost perfect iron . What 's wrong with it ? The lovely , dark blue body makes it almost impossible to see the water level within the iron . It is hard to gauge how much is left and if you need to add more . The only other negative is its pictorial manual . I realize that pictures practically eliminate the need for text and that , to be sold in countries with speakers of languages other than English , this should be a good way to communicate with a variety of users . But , the black and white graphics are small , and there are too many squeezed onto each page . The overall effect is plain frustration . I would much prefer a larger manual , containing both larger graphics and text . Those negatives aside , this is a wonderful iron . It heats very rapidly and steams powerfully . The weight is well balanced . It 's heavy enough to smooth out wrinkles and light enough to be user friendly . The Teflon coated plate makes using starch much easier , as it does n't stick to cloth . Less sticking and smooth gliding make for a faster job . Cleaning a Teflon coated surface is easier than cleaning a metal one . The opening of the iron 's water well is narrow . This iron comes with a narrow measuring cup that easily allows you to pour water into the cavity of the iron , eliminating spills and water marks on fabrics . My previous iron was a Black and Decker . I like this one better . Its ability to heat rapidly and steam like a commercial laundry make it a winner .

text: Purchased this model less than a year ago -LRB- 2010 -RRB- . Did so based on the Black & Decker name . The price was great and had the auto-off feature I felt was very important . text: A decade or so , I bought this same iron , and it was GREAT . It was a simple , no nonsense iron that just worked . text: I despise ironing with a passion . I 've owned several irons , some of them fairly expensive , and this is the first one that can properly iron a shirt pane or collar in one pass , which considerably reduces the amount of time and effort it takes to do the job . The D6000 weighs 4 lbs , which is quite heavy but probably explains why it does such a good job ; it glides effortlessly over most materials . Unlike most other irons where you set the temperature using a mechanical dial , the D6000 features a fancy blue backlit LCD control panel displaying all the settings -LRB- steam , temperature , fabric type ... -RRB- in an easy to read , large font . The steam is abundant and constant regardless of the temperature setting as advertised , a very useful feature . Of course , the flipside is that you get to fill up the water tank more often , which fortunately is a snap thanks to the large fill port : hold the iron horizontally , open the fill port and put under the water faucet . A `` vertical steam '' mode lets you steam hanging clothes and drapes -LRB- I have not had a chance to use this feature -RRB- The manufacturer states that the iron does not need to be emptied after each use . The self cleaning feature should be used once a month to insure proper descaling . The D6000 is easy to use and almost reconciled me with the ironing chore . By cutting my ironing time in half , it has more than earned its five stars .
label: Iron

text: I was looking for an iron to replace a Rowenta that I dropped ... although I was not entirely unhappy that I needed to buy a new iron as the Rowenta was not nearly as good as my prior iron , a Norelco . I read up on the reviews of the Panasonic iron that I am reviewing and given the cost I figured I was not risking much . I have used the iron for a few weeks and at this point I would rate it as good but not great . It produces plenty of steam which I assumed was the key to getting tough wrinkles out of a garment . Unfortunately that is not the case apparently as the Panasonic is no miracle worker as far as removing tough wrinkles ... again , good but not great at this task . The iron has a nice feel to it and is not light nor heavy . I do n't actually know what the key to getting wrinkles out - could it be that the Panny just does not get hot enough to smooth out those stubborn wrinkles ? Over the holidays I was visiting family and they had a Black and Decker iron that did a great job of removing wrinkles . It was an older model but did a really nice job of ironing garments . And as noted above the Norelco I had years ago was a stellar performer , but cost probably at least double what the Panny retails for . Anyway , I 'm happy but not thrilled with this product .

text: I like the way this iron does the job but it is made too top heavy and falls forward when I put it down . The shape is a little odd but it does press out wrinkles very well .

text: I have to confess right off that I am a quilter . So I have two requirements for an iron , that it heat up fast and that I can use it to lightly glide over the cloth I am working with to keep it from stretching . This particular iron meets both needs perfectly . In fact , the bottom surface of the iron is almost glass-like . To say it irons smoothly is a little bit like saying that the north pole gets a bit cool in the winter . This baby is actually slick ! It uses regular water , and is designed to remove the impurities , unlike my last iron -LRB- a much older model , I confess -RRB- , which needed distilled water to ensure long term usage . To save paper -LRB- and possibly to avoid linguistic issues -RRB- a majority of the instructions are pictorial . However , the location of all of the settings was pretty standard , and it only took me a few seconds to figure everything out . I do like the automatic turn off feature , especially since it does heat up so fast . Again , as a quilter I often go back and forth between sewing and ironing , so I am more used to simply leaving the iron on . My last one had a longer period before it shut off , so I was a bit worried . I occasionally iron some things , and this works well for that occasional piece that needs a touch up when I pull it out of the dryer . Very nice for my use !

text: Great iron -LRB- Black & Decker D6000 -RRB- , sturdy , professional look on mens dress shirts , great steam power . text: 3.5 out of 5 stars . Overall , this is a good iron for everyday tasks . What I like best is that the burst of steam is powerful and that the cotton setting is hot enough to do a nice job on 100 % cotton . I like the oversized heel that prevents the iron from tipping easily when it is set vertically on my padded ironing board . I like that the water container is transparent , not just a small transparent window , so it is easy to see how full the iron is . What I noticed first of the negative aspects is the comfort of the handle . It felt awkward in my hand as soon as I picked it up , and when I ironed for a significant period of time without a break , it was uncomfortable . The water nozzle sprays too horizontally , so I end up spraying the wall behind the ironing board instead of the fabric I 'm trying to dampen , and this was a strong negative for me . The instructions are terrible . Tiny pictures with slashes through them were pretty much indecipherable for me . Use your words , people . And while instructions are not terribly important for irons , it does help to be able to use them for explanation of any special features . I do n't care for the heat adjustment dial . It is under the handle , as it is on many other types of irons , and it is awkward to use there , and not very precise . My old iron had several more settings for different fabrics , and this one pretty much leaves it up to your best guess . I would like to be able to use the burst of steam more often rather than waiting between uses , but if used too often , there is water instead of steam dribbled on the fabric . This is pretty typical of irons , though , at least in my experience . The iron does go through quite a bit of water while using the steam bursts , so it would have been nice if the reservoir had been a bit bigger , but that is n't a significant negative for me . It is nice that it does n't have to be used with distilled water , that tap water is fine . If you choose this iron , be sure to iron for a few minutes first on a piece of fabric that is n't important to you . There were specks and dirty-looking bits on the fabric when I first started ironing , and my guess is that these are leftover from the manufacturing process , but they were something I was not expecting at all . Overall , this is a good iron for most people 's uses , but quilters and sewers who use an iron for multiple hours daily might find it a bit lacking .
label: Iron

text: This iron is a nice replacement for the one we 've had since getting married 8 years ago , sure , but it 's not ideal either . I suppose for the price point , this iron is a pretty good value . When I first plugged this iron in , it heated immediately , so that 's definitely a nice feature . It 's a nice weight and feels good in my hand . It does n't seem to glide as well as my old iron , but I do love the extra sharp point on this iron -- especially since I use irons more for crafts & sewing than I do for pressing clothes . Being able to steam a garment upright is also a nice feature . Filling the iron is easy enough once you understand the instructions . I do have a bit of an issue with the instructions , I 'd prefer more words than just tons of little pictures demonstrating how to operate the iron . There were a couple images that seemed almost identical , except one had a `` NO ! '' and one looking almost just like it said , `` Yes . '' Reading the manual for this iron is like playing one of those `` find the differences '' games in the back of a magazine . As far as the actual ironing job this product does , it 's good . The dial is located behind the handle , which I do n't like because it seems in the way and I have to lift and look through the handle to see what setting I 'm putting the iron on . The dial is n't labeled very clear , either , and the arrow that marks the setting is small and hard to see . But like I said , an iron is and iron and as far as pressing clothes this one did a good job . My only other complaint is that the cord does not wind up easily around the iron . My old iron -LRB- as well as many other 's I 've seen in stores -RRB- feature grooves that allow for easy cord wrapping . Or best yet , some have a retractable cord -- which is so nice when appliances feature this ! This iron , however , lacks both . I would recommend it to someone who needs a good basic iron that gets the job done , but is n't picky about design . It functions just fine , it 's just not well designed in my opinion . I think the price does make this iron a good value , when all is said and done . Some people pay a couple hundred bucks for an iron , if these are the setbacks I have to deal with to only have to spend a fraction of that -- then I 'm good with this iron .
label: Iron

text: The T-fal Prima Iron has a lot of features that I was lacking from my old iron I received 8 years ago as a wedding gift . I am extremely forgetful and I have in the past for brief moments forgotten the iron and once ruined one of my husband 's shirts . The auto off feature is really quite amazing . If it does n't sense any movement when left flat , it will turn itself off in 30 seconds . We moms know how crazy things can become in a moment 's notice and our attention is often divided in several different directions . For times like this , the auto off is literally a life saver . It will also turn off automatically if left upright for 8 minutes . Its nice to have the peace of mind that if I do happen to walk away and forget it , it will take care of itself . I also noticed right away how easily the prima iron glides on all types of fabric . I often made even more creases with my old iron when the iron got caught up on something . Also gone are all the messy drips that used to annoy me to no end . The prima iron has an anti drip feature that prevents leaking when the iron has n't yet reached the temperatures to create the steam . The design is also a nice improvement , being much less prone to tipping while upright . Overall , it 's a great buy for an iron in this price range , much better than others of a similar price that you might find at your local retailer .

text: Ok , first off lets get the bad in my opinion out of the way . The instructions are terrible . Its all small circular photos about the size of a nickel . I guess T-Fal figures that people are brite enough to figure it all out on their own . The iron itself is a nice sleek stylish looking appliance . I love the blue color ! The handle has a rubber grip which helps keep it firm in your hand . There is a nice size temperature dial that clicks as you turn it . It does n't have numbers on it to show temps but a few icons . Most clothing labels have a temp so you know how to to make your iron . I start off using the middle of the dial and do a small test on the fabric i 'm ironing to see if its too hot . There 's a number of options on the handle like steam burst and also water spray . You can also change the intensity of the steam burst with 3 settings like off , and min. , max . . Theres a pop out plug\/cap for filling the iron with water . I read that using distilled water is best because there are no minerals in it that will clog the iron over time . It also comes with a small pitcher to make it easy filling up the iron rather than sticking it under the faucet . The cord is pretty long and also has a slight swivel to it which helps it stand upright when not in use . But the best feature is the no-stick surface . Its pretty much exactly like the no-stick surface found on their cookware . I found that ironing is smooth and very easy compared to my old sunbeam from 25 yrs ago . I do think this iron is a little heavier than my older one but its not an issue . Safety is also important and this iron has 3 auto off features two are 30 seconds if left down or if it falls on its side . The other is in its correct upright position will shut off in 8 minutes if not used . I hope this iron lasts as long as my older one because I really like it . -LRB- wanted a no-stick iron as my other one is not -RRB-

text:
I bought this keyboard as a replacement for the MS Elite model , expecting to make heavy use of the macro keys . But it turns out that the Pro is n't a worthwhile replacement for several reasons , and in fact I could n't even stand it long enough to bother reprogramming the macro keys .

The most important problem to me is the feel of the keyboard . I think MS must have done something to cut their costs , and quality , because this thing feels nothing like the comfortable Elite . I must say I 'm very disappointed . The Elite was quiet and extremely solid , but the Pro feels hollow and flimsy , as if is has the guts of a keyboard . But there are other less-important annoyances too .

The Pro has a full-sized set of arrow key , but I usually use the numeric pad with numlock off anways -LRB- so I can immediately touch Home , End , Enter , Ins , Del , etc. -RRB- , so that 's just farther for my arm to travel between the keypad , mouse , and home row . Finally , the Pro 's footprint is huge . I ca n't stress that enough ! I even have a giant desktop , and this thing is still a pain to reach around just to use my mouse or drawing tablet . The Elite had a tiny footprint , and I never even realized how nice that was until I lost it .

I 've tried out \* lots \* of ergonomic keyboards in my time , but the MS Elite is still the best by far . So I 'd say to save some money , and get a better keyboard too .

Everything else is amazing . Buy this . I should have bought it sooner . My wrists are happy : -RRB-

text: I have an HTC HD2 running ChuckyRom Winmo and DarkStone Froyo2 .2 with Cyanogenmod 6.1 . In Winmo the keyboard works as intended and does essentially what I want it to do . However , when I boot into Android -LRB- which I use as my main OS -RRB- , the keyboard does not work . The product comes with a CD and what it leads you to believe are the necessary drivers to run the keyboard . I installed the program and then tried running it , only to repeatedly get an error saying the one time registration had failed , and the program closed . After some searching on Google I finally find that the manufacturer has n't renewed their license with the company that provides the software drivers for the phone . After spending 28 dollars on the keyboard , I find that I now have to pay an additional 10 euros to download the software that the manufacturer claimed would come with the product . Additionally , I have n't been able to get the keyboard functioning on an Android build that does n't have Cyanogenmod 6.1 installed , so if you 're running a stock phone it seems like you 're S.O.L. there too Be aware of this product and the countless other `` mini bluetooth keyboards '' that are seemingly made by different manufacturers but are all exactly the same product . Using multiple brands for the same product is usually a sign of a disreputable company . As for the keyboard itself , it 's very light and feels sort of cheap , but it is sturdy enough . The power switch is a bit difficult to slide , and the mini-USB port does n't seem to be very well attached . My power light flickers and turns off frequently while charging , and wiggling the wire around usually brings it back on for a while . Overall , this is a very cheaply made product , but unfortunately it seems like its the only bluetooth keyboard of its size available at the moment for a reasonable price . If you are running Android , I strongly suggest you DO NOT PURCHASE THIS PRODUCT .
label: Keyboard

text: Let me start out by saying that I 'm not disappointed at all with this purchase -- but neither am I totally satisfied either . And since this short string has no real purpose , I do n't consider it basic at all . Sure , it makes the stylus harder to lose , but that 's about its only redeeming quality . And of course , it is removable , but then you have an odd little silver post with a hole in it on the top of the stylus . Finally there is the tip . It 's nice and soft , but I 'm not sure that 's a good thing . It seems that you either need to press harder than I would really like , or upon touching the screen hold it still for a beat before moving in order to get it to register . Again , not bad , just not quite good either . Overall , I 'll continue to use this stylus as it gets the job done . However , I 'm going to be on the lookout for something better . text: AmazonBasics has quickly become a ` brand ' I trust . Between the various cables , cards , and occasional computer accessory that I 've used from them , they just provide an excellent value . Great products at great prices . This stylus is no exception . It 's simple , effective , and convenient . I am very happy with my purchase ; something I wish I could say more often . It works . It works well . It works on just about everything I 've tried it on . It works on my Kindle Touch -LRB- saving it from a greasy-fingerprinted fate -RRB- . It works on my smartphone -LRB- saving me the frustration of somehow hitting four buttons at once with the tip of my deceptively clumsy finger -RRB- . It even works with those sorry P.o.S. card scanners at the grocery store where you have to attempt to sign your name without looking like you did it with your foot . It works on every touch device I 've tried -- with precision that I could not get with my fingers or lesser styli . It 's convenient . Being just a bit shorter than a cheap-o ball point pen , and about the same diameter , it goes easily into and out of a pocket . It also feels familiarly comfortable in the hand . -LRB- For reference , I 've got fairly big hands and the size is sufficient to hold onto ; I 'd even say perfect , as it 's only as long as it needs to be . I wish they made pens \* this \* size , instead of longer -- this stylus never stabs me in the groin when I sit down with it in my pocket . -RRB- Carrying it with you is easy enough , and it 's dimensions allow you to swipe a cap off of an old pen to protect the tip with no fear of damaging it in your pocket\/purse . It 's well-built . The stylus is solid . I have no fear of buckling the shaft or losing parts . After two months of carrying it around unprotected in my pockets with my keys it has no chips , no scratches , no problems . I am impressed . I was initially worried about the little rubber tip , but it seems surprisingly resilient -LRB- though I 've not gone out of my way to damage it , either -RRB- . Now that I keep a cap on it , it 's virtually indestructible under ` normal pocket conditions ' . I did end up taking the little lanyard off of it , because I would always accidentally pull the stylus out of my pocket when fishing for my keys ... So I put the lanyard on my keys and can always get them easily now . It 's like I got a little bonus item with my stylus . = -RRB- It 's a great value . Honestly , I did n't think I 'd get this much use out of a stylus . I mean , I 've got fingers ! All the time ! I do n't \* need \* a stylus , right ? Well , let me put it this way : I felt pretty dumb the other day when I went searching through my pockets for this little guy , did n't find it , got up , walked downstairs , searched yesterday 's pants , found it , walked all the way back up stairs , picked up my Kindle ... and then I realized that I am apparently becoming an idiot . This stylus has become the primary form of interaction with touch screens for me . I never really used 'em much before , but now I 'm lost without it . And you know what ? It was less than 10 bucks . I ca n't think of many things that I use so often -LRB- and am so happy with -RRB- for just 10 bucks . And I can use it with pretty much all of my devices . This thing was made for a specific purpose , and it works beautifully for that purpose . I do n't think that 's too much to ask , but it 's pretty uncommon these days . I wish more of my purchases ended up being this satisfying .

text: I 've only had a chance to use the Tactikka Plus on one 6 mile run . So far I like it very much . It 's very lightweight , comfortable and stays in place . Some of the roads I run on are very well lit while others are almost completely dark . I wanted a light that was bright enough for me to see in the dark places even after my eyes had adjusted to the street-lit areas . The packaging for mine did not have the `` 80 % Brighter '' sticker that I have seen on similar new Petzl equipment so I wondered if it was going to work well for me . It did . Even at the low setting it was bright enough for me to see potholes and dips in the road . The light covers a wide area and reaches far enough in front that I do n't `` outrun '' it . The switch is easy to use . The adjustable angle housing is essential for dialing in exactly where you want the light pointed without having to hold your head at a weird angle . The headband does soak up sweat and it did take a few hours to dry out . I do n't know if anything can be done to improve this . Overall , I would recommend it to others . I would also check out the Princeton Tec Quad . It looks to be nearly identical but it is cheaper . It trades the flip up red lens on the Petzl for a waterproof housing . \*\*\* UPDATE \*\*\* I 've now had this headlamp for 5 months and use it many times per week . Everything I liked about it initially I still like . For no apparent reason the lamp now will not turn off . Instead it goes into an ultra dim mode that you may not notice at first but continues to drain the batteries . I have not dropped or physically damaged the light . I 've tried new batteries twice . Then I sent the lamp back to Petzl at my expense for their review . Customer service was excellent and prompt . They sent me a new lamp but unfortunately it is a Tactikka instead of a Tactikka Plus which means it only turns on or off . This is a shame because I use the lamp almost exclusively in the low setting . While my old lamp was being evaluated I used a Princton Tec Fuel . Unfortunately for Petzl even though this lamp has only three LED 's it is an improvement over my Tactikka plus in every way except comfort . It is only slightly less comfortable to wear and the flood pattern is better -LRB- it has more of a spotlight in the center with a wider flood light on the sides -RRB- . I ended up never using the red flip up filter on the Tactikka Plus because I wanted to make sure oncoming traffic could see me easily . I 'll be using the Fuel instead of the Tacktikka now .
label: Lamp

text: I recently purchased both this headlamp , and a similar model from Energizer , -LRB- Energizer 6 LED Headlight -RRB- and my experiences have been quite different with each . This is my first Amazon review , so bear with me : First , the price . The Petzl 4-LED headlamp is $ 30 plus , whereas the Energizer 6-LED is approximately $ 20 . More light for less cash . Score one for Energizer . Secondly , the construction . The plastic housing used on the Petzl model seems far less rugged than the Energizer . In further comparison , the elastic headband just seems cheaply produced . Then there 's the battery compartment . It 's a bit awkward , and took me a few minutes to get the housing to close again properly . The lamp itself does have a smaller body than its cousin , but not by much . Finally , the functionality . The Petzl model 's swivel is notched and gives four angles of possible illumination . The Energizer model 's swivel is smooth , and gives what I 'd approximate to be 120 degrees of light . The Petzl might give 40 degrees . The red filter is just a cheap flap of red translucent plastic . Really not much to say about that . The reason I give this product three stars is because it does what it 's supposed to do . It 'll give you a decent hands-free lighting option wether you 're in the woods or trying to fix your car on the side of the road . I 'm not affiliated with Energizer , but as the title says , there are better options for your money .

text: Let me start by saying , I have several head lamps from several different manufacturers - and I like this one the best ! I have some that are `` rated '' to put out more lumens , but , they are not necessarily better . This Petzl lamp puts out the most `` usable '' light , in a great flood pattern , with very few dark spots , or excessive bright spots - which in the end , I found to be the most valuable as an ` all around headlamp ' . This lamp works great for biking and jogging , that 's probably were it excel 's best . I tend to use mine more when I am working on projects with tools , and I need more light . It 's vastly superior to a flashlight , in that it automatically points where ever you are looking , and it 's hands free -LRB- of course , if you are trying to talk to someone you will blind them if you look at them - that is the one draw back to these kind of lamps -RRB- . This headlamp uses common size batteries -LRB- pretty important , I have a nice Fenix light that takes the CR123A batteries , which are expensive and somewhat hard to find in a pinch -RRB- and this Petzl headlamp has excellent battery life ! Another very nice feature is that you press the button once for on -LRB- high setting -RRB- , and after a few seconds it is set so the next button push is off . Compare that with some of the older models and different brands that you have to push the button 4 or 5 times to go throught the different brightness scales and the flashing mode to get to the off position . If you did want to use one of the lower power settings , you just push the button 2 , 3 , or 4 times when you first turn it on , get to the setting you want - then after a few seconds of use , it reverts back to the next button push is off . You may be thinking to yourself , `` big deal '' , but I think you will find that this is a pretty nice feature . With my older lamps , I hated scrolling through all those modes everytime I want to turn it on and off . Personally , I find the red lens valueless . I am sure there is someone that finds the red lens valuable , when they are hunting , or other such thing . But for a city guy like me , I just do n't have a need for it , so I just leave it down and out of the way . However , I will mention that it works fine , just like it is supposed to , no sticking or problems with the lens , and it does a good job , exactly as you would expect . The shipping for this product was good , timely -LRB- fast , 2 days -RRB- , and the package arrived in perfect condition as did the products inside the package . I have no complaints . I am confident when I say , that if you take everything into consideration - brightness , battery life , easy of use , reliability , quality of light , number of dark spots , size of flood illumination , comfort of the strap , the fact that it uses common every day batteries -- all of the little nuances and add them up -- you will find that this is , if not the best headlamp out there today , it is certainly in the top 3 .

text: Careful here . Sellers of this product profit from sales to unsuspecting customers . The Sony product utilizes a Phillips lamp . The seller in the instance of my purchase does not mention that the Phillips lamp has been removed from his product and a lamp of unknown origin has been installed . In order to do so , the seal between the two housing halves is broken . However , the product warranty supplied states that if the seal is broken , seller will not honor warranty . So , buyer receives a non-Sony lamp within a Sony housing -LRB- which housing adds nothing to the product function , as the old one is perfectly fine -RRB- -- and should the lamp of unknown origin fail , or provide substandard performance , as some reviewers have noted , the buyer can be denied warranty coverage because seller has broken the seal to remove -- and likely resell -- the proper Phillips lamp , in the process of replacing that original , Sony-supplied lamp with the one he ships to you .

text: If you 're considering switching to a Mac , I 'd like to share my experience and its happy ending . Others have sufficiently reviewed and critiqued the Macbook Air from a technical point of view ... so I wo n't reiterate that . Instead I 'll briefly recount the thought process and experience of one guy making the transition . I debated for a long time about whether to make the switch from my Windows based laptop to the Mac platform ... I had bought my wife a Mac Mini two years ago and she has actually felt so-so about it . Her Mac is far more stable than her previous PC , which she appreciates . But -- she feels -- her Mac is not as amazing and `` intuitive '' as it was cracked up to be . We have discovered in the meanwhile that there is some truth to the idea that some people 's brains just seem to resonate more with one system or the other . In many ways , she is more of a `` Windows gal , '' as she puts it . Nonetheless , she has been very happy to have a system that is so stable and does not crash or freeze . And in most other respects , she has been satisfied . So given her sufficiently positive experience , it made sense to seriously consider the idea of getting our entire household all on the same platform rather than having to duplicate software and OS upgrades moving forward . Furthermore , I was increasingly having several issues with my laptop : slow-ish ; prone to daily freezes or crashes ; weird , buggy quirks that I could never seem to eliminate . Finally , being a -LRB- very -RRB- frequent business traveler , the weight of it -LRB- about 6-7 pounds , with the power supply -RRB- , was a big drag . So given all these considerations , it was a natural to consider the switch . So why did n't I make the leap right away ??? My hesitation stemmed from several sources . 1st , I 'd spent a chunk of money just a few years back on my laptop and its docking station -- so the `` Scotsman '' in me was not too keen to switch to a new machine before thoroughly `` amortizing '' my previous investment , i.e. getting my full money 's worth out of it ! 2nd , the thought of having to transfer files , re-load software , re-establish internet and email settings , etc. etc. made me nauseous to even think about it ... Finally , there would be some things on my Windows laptop that I would n't be able to use on my new Macbook Air -LRB- like receipt scanner software that goes with my Windows-based travel scanner ... or my MS Flight simulator and joystick -RRB- . In sum , making the leap entailed some significant costs , monetary and otherwise . Ultimately , after thinking through all the pros and cons , it boiled down to : -LRB- a -RRB- erase my laptop and reinstall XP -LRB- or purchase Windows 7 -RRB- plus all other software to get all the performance issues resolved or -LRB- b -RRB- bite the bullet , pay the bucks , and make the switch . In the end , the idea of running two platforms in one house just did n't make sense ... and longer-term , it seemed like a fruitless struggle to keep myself chained to an inferior system ... And , amidst that joyless scenario , I kept hearing fantastic kudos from friends and colleagues about the Macbook Air -LRB- MBA -RRB- and othe Macs ... making it very alluring , indeed ... I did briefly consider the Macbook Pros ... but I did n't really need any extra processing power that that decision might entail ... and being a road warrior clinched it . The light weight , instant turn-on and turn-off of the MBA -LRB- plus the fact that I use fairly basic functions , like internet , email , Office , iPhoto , etc. -RRB- made me opt for the Macbook Air -LRB- MBA -RRB- , which I purchased in July 2011 . I was , of course , chagrined to have them come out with the NEXT model just a month later ... but the differences are fairly negligible , given my usage . I did buy an external disk drive for the very occasional time when I might need it . In 2 months , I 've only needed it 1 time , thus far . So not having to schlepp that embedded weight around is actually a huge plus . Similarly having `` only '' 2 USB ports has proven to be a non-issue . I have to switch out some cords now and again , but it is no big deal compared to the extra weight and clunkiness more ports would entail . The transition from PC to Mac has been far easier than I anticipated all in all ... and though not everything about the way a Mac functions is better or more efficient than Windows -LRB- sometimes there are some surprising , but minor , quirks and deficiencies -RRB- , overall , I do indeed find the Mac experience , stability , and the fabled `` intuitiveness '' to be a joy . I just love this computer and it was worth every penny . I 've never said that about any previous computer I 've owned . And as light as it is , I actually use it around home just like an iPad ... yet can do serious work on it . So , if you 're at all similar to me -LRB- 50 ; long-time PC user ; critical ; appreciative of systems and equipment which work effectively and aesthetically ; fairly basic computer user -LRB- though Mac is known for working wonderfully for far more sophisticated functions than I need -RRB- ... then life is too short not to make the switch . It really is a joy !

text: This is my first Mac . I was considering buying a Dell XPS but after doing research and realizing that I did n't really need a blu ray player -LRB- since I have a PS3 -RRB- the Mac was a better choice for me . I 'm an architecture student so this i7 Macbook Pro was a no brainer . I went from a 14.5 '' Dell Inspiron to this 15.4 '' Mac and was afraid it would be to bulky but it is just perfect ! It is a bit heavy , -LRB- specially with the Speck cover -RRB- nothing compared to other computers but do n't think it 's light as a feather . I was afraid the operating system would be hard to get use to since I 've used Windows all my life but with all the tutorials at the apple website I got the hang of it in no time ! It 's very intuitive ! I read many reviews which complained about how hot this mac gets , so I was really worried about that . After testing it doing renders while working on Photoshop I really did n't notice any difference from my old Dell Inspiron . I do have a Speck protective case on it , maybe that helps with the heat ? Nevertheless the heat `` problem '' does n't bother me at all because like I said , it does get hot but there is really no difference from my old laptop and other laptops I 've used . I do agree with some reviewers that point out that the front edges dig into your wrists and are kind of annoying but I got use to it after awhile . It bothers me that Apple has n't added an HDMI port on their high-end models but a 15 $ adapter did the trick . They also modified the charger . The cables look and feel a lot stronger from previous versions . The battery does n't last 8 hours like advertised but it 's still better than normal computers in this range . Having the screen on constantly -LRB- using the app `` caffeine '' -RRB- at medium brightness , wifi on and the keyboard light off it lasts about 5 hours . Most important of all , its FAST . Working on 3D models is a breeze now ! Renders that would take me 1 or 2 hours in my old laptop take only five minutes in this incredible machine ! Bottom line , it 's a great computer and it 's worth the price . I do n't know why I waited so long .
label: Laptop

text: This stand addresses the ergonomics of using your laptop , and this is the feature that I like the most about this product . I can use the computer on my lap , on the desk , on a kitchen counter , etc. and adjust the height of the screen to nearly eye level . I can work longer and more comfortably at my computer now . It cools my laptop well , where my loud internal fan rarely ever comes on , unlike prior to this stand where it ran often . It is well made with thick plastic and a metal bracket for adjusting the height of the stand . The speed of the fan is adjustable and can be turned completely off with a switch . The fan can be heard at full speed but it is still much quieter than my internal laptop fan was at full speed . Even at full speed , this fan is quiet , and that is where I leave mine . Some reviewers felt that the laptop holders near the bottom of the stand got in the way while typing , but they do n't interfere with my typing position . There is a narrow and wide position for them , and the narrow position keeps them out of my way , if sitting up . Also , I know from trying another stand -LRB- link below -RRB- that if these holders are n't large enough to curve over the upper edge of the laptop -LRB- like these do -RRB- , then the laptop will slide up and over them when the screen is angled back for proper viewing . So , these slightly larger holders make this a much more stable and usable product if you keep your laptop at any position other than the 2 lowest settings . Also , if you do put the stand at a high angle , you will probably want to use an external keyboard and mouse , since your keyboard will be at about a 45 degree angle when at the highest setting . This angle is fine for using the trackpad , but harder on your wrists for a lot of typing . The base has large non-skid pads , is not cushioned and is meant for hard surfaces , but I still use mine mostly on my lap . I had tried the Connectland CL-NBK68001 Swivel Base 15-Inch Laptop Stand and Cooler , Metal Grill With Fan prior to the Cooler Master , and it was not a good fit for me since I mainly wanted to adjust the height of my laptop . The bottom brackets which held the computer on the stand when it was at a high angle were too small , allowing the bottom half of the computer to slide over them and off . It did have a strong , very quiet fan . It was smaller and lighter than this cooler master stand , but it did not feel as rugged as the cooler master . If you want a stand for cooling , not raising your laptop screen for ergonomics , then this may be a better stand for you since the brackets are smaller making it is easier to type on than the cooler master .
label: Laptop

text:
Lots of problems \/ deficiencies mainly that the firewire does not work reliably and no USB2 alternative . Creative support has been utterly useless . Even though using creative audigy they suggest that I buy a different firewire card or turn off USB since `` it is known to conflict with firewire '' . Lots of people are reporting same issue . Mine is being returned .

- No digital audio out - Inadequate volume whether using supplied -LRB- low quaility -RRB- headphones or Sennheisers . - Slow , slow , slow . Takes ~ 20 minutes to boot from total power out w \/ just 3100 mp 's - Playlist only displays 20 chars of title . So with a lot of band names you wo n't be able to read any of the song titles to select what you want to play . - One EAX effect at a time . IMO it sounds best with advanced eq on meaning you ca n't have smart volume management on . The latest firmware claims to address problems with this but I have seen little evidence . It does appear you can have EQ on and enable SVM from playcenter -LRB- not from player menu 's -RRB- . The EQ is definitely on but I have seen no evidence SVM is working . - The use of a proprietary disk format such that it is not recognized as a drive in Windows . The filemanager and playcenter simply -LRB- are bad -RRB- IMO . - Inclusion of a USB 1 connection rather than one compatible with 1 & 2 . Firewire is included but the implementation is VERY buggy and failed at least 10 times during the transfer of 3100 MP3 's . That included blue screening XP once with the included playcenter\/driver version . The latest does not crash XP but the failures still occur . When they do XP has to be rebooted for it to ever work again AND it totally locks up the player requiring total power off -LRB- battery removal -RRB- . When it does that it must rebuild the file list again which as mentioned above is VERY ... slow . Get used to the `` device not connected or busy message '' . - It rejected as invalid mp3 's , during the transfer , about 20 even though several other players including winamp and win media player have no problem with them . The real problem is the idiotic dialog box that comes up making an unattended transfer impossible - Overall the menu system is absolutely horrible IMO . - Playback is pretty good but I 've noticed some strange behavior like sometimes just jumping to the next song before the current has finished . Along with that some other weird behavior like a song repetitively skipping like a CD does when dirty , or noises during playback not in the MP3 . - You do n't get 20 gigs of music storage as advertised . It 's really 19056mb and the number of songs -LRB- 8000 -RRB- is a bit exaggerated and misleading . With 3100 MP3 's mostly in the 128-160 range , including ~ 15 full CD 's and some 192 's & 320 's the drive is less than 3 gigs short of full . I have not done the math yet but I suspect it is not really a true 20gb drive -LRB- 20480 -RRB- but rather that they do the math like Dell and some other misleading companies where they choose to define 1k as 1000 rather than the correct 1024 bytes . Just a. . . marketing ploy that I believe Dell started some time ago . That leaves about 1.4 gb missing from a `` 20gb '' drive and I doubt formatting or software used it up .

text: If I could , I would have given it 4.5 stars as it is n't PERFECT . Been using it for about a week now , my comments ... Pros : 1 . Case will keep it scratch free and clean . 2 . High quality audio . I do not hear the hiss people talk about and it has no problems getting loud with no distortion . Also has user adjustable equalizer . I find reducing the 1K band by 3db sounds better for me . 3 . The knob is easy to use . Do n't listen to people saying it 's no good because it is `` buried in a pit '' . 4 . Tons of features - FM , voice record , inline recording , multi-format support . There are many things you can tweak in the menus . 5 . USB 2.0 is a must when transferring a lot of data . 6 . 1 gig is currently as big as it gets for flash players . Shake it ALL you want , it wo n't skip and you do n't have to worry about a failed drive like in the iPod . 7 . Replaceable AA that should last about 40 hours . No need to worry about an expensive lithium . Cons : 1 . UMS is now available with firmware 1.28 . There are three caveats to the 1.28 release though : Your maximum record bitrate is 96Kpbs . With 1.25 , it is 320Kbps . You can not directly edit you radio station lists and names -LRB- this can be done on the player though -RRB- . Finally , the order of the folders and files can not be changed by the user . Most people wo n't have issue with this , but I thought I 'd list them out . I currently am using 1.25 with the iriver software as it is rare that I need to connect to it and I prefer my list to be in order . BTW , it works with WMP for transfer if you do n't want to user their software . 2 . It will not allow upload of MP3 files -LRB- you ca n't transfer the files off the player after you put them on -RRB- . This does not mean you ca n't store things on the device , simply change the extension name . 3 . Comes with Sennheiser ear buds . They are unequal length - some people like it , some do n't . The longer line is designed to go behind the head - I actually like that . Their quality is better then average , but are not terribly comfortable in my ear . From the minor research I 've done , the Beyerdynamic DT 231 seems to be an excellent set for the price . Try http:\/\/www.headphone.com . 4 . Requires custom USB cable , does n't just plug right in . Ca n't really complain though . My opinion on some gripes people have : 1 . The buttons are easy to hit accidently - I have not had issue . I use the case all the time and have not hit any buttons by accident yet . 2 . Slow display - It will scroll pretty slowly by default , but this can be sped up in the menu . 3 . Inline jack next to microphone jack - Let 's face it , this is not an issue . Simply remember that the MC jack is on the left when the joystick is pointing up . Even if you get it wrong , it wo n't blow it up . 4 . USB door is weak - I 'm not certain if it will weaken over time , but it clicks shut and holds with no issue thus far . 5 . The band - I have n't used it and I do n't think I will . I 'd probably hold it or place it in my pocket if you are running or exercising . - Alex

text: UPDATE : Summary - Not recommended . Application : I use this as a music server in my car . Other MP3 players I use : iPod Shuffle -LRB- 2nd generation , 1 GB -RRB- for running , used almost daily . iPod Touch -LRB- 3rd generation , 32 MB -RRB- , used more for business apps and eReader than for music . Pros : Great capacity in a tiny size . Intuitive navigation . Easy one-handed use . The `` home '' button was a stroke of genius . Display is clear and legible in almost any lighting condition . Great sound , better than my iPod Shuffle , even with the stock earbuds . -LRB- Actually , the stock Sansa earbuds made my Shuffle sound better . -RRB- Fairly easy to manage with Windows Media Player . Voice recorder is great for making notes on-the-go . The expandable memory was a bonus . Cons : Two bigs and one small , adding up to fatal weakness . Big Negative # 1 is that the design of the actual clip component exceeds the limits of what can be done with plastic and a metal spring . I did n't wear it often , and certainly never clipped it onto anything that caused a strain , but one of the two attachment points broke within the first half-dozen clip uses -LRB- in other words , within the first month -RRB- . Also , even when new , the clip was pretty weak - much less grabby than the iPod Shuffle . If I wanted a clip-on player for active wear , I 'd look elsewhere . UPDATE : The clip broke entirely off after a few months . Very , very poor design here . UPDATE : Big Negative # 2 is that it lost its little mind . Although it can still play music , it is now totally unrecognized by any of five computers in the house , running either Vista or XP with all service packs and updates applied . I can no longer add music to it . SanDisk is no help . The smaller negative is that the shuffle mode does n't seem to shuffle as thoroughly as the iPods do - I get a lot of repeats and sequential songs by the same artist or off the same album . Notes : As an in-car music server connected to the car 's sound system , it works well , although if I had to do it over I might get something with a larger display ; this one is small for my weak eyes . It replaces a big stack of CDs with something I can hide in the ashtray . UPDATE : I should have spent just a bit more and gotten a refurbed Nano for the larger display and better build quality .
label: MP3 Player

text: This is my first MP3 player and I chose it for the following reasons : it is PC compatible ; has an FM tuner ; and iRiver gets excellent ratings for sound quality . But the deciding factor was that I loved the way it looked in `` Trance Red '' -LRB- not `` Trace Red '' -RRB- - like a lacquered cigarette or small jewelry case . In three weeks of use , I have not been disappointed . Media Player 10 handles the synchronization flawlessly . The real payoff is that I finally have the motivation to collect , categorize and listen to ALL my music , not just the six CDs I have in the car . For me that means going all the way back to the 60s . The fact that this little jewel holds it all -LRB- for now -RRB- , operates seamlessly and can plug into a home stereo , a car audio system or be taken to the gym or on a trip completely changes the way I listen to music . The FM tuner gets the best reception I 've experienced in portable unit . Be prepared to spend another $ 50 to $ 100 for better earphones -LRB- an unending search -RRB- and connectivity accessories . A few negatives . First , the repeat-pause-skip buttons on the side provide no tactile feedback to differentiate one from another and they do not react the first time around . So you if the H10 is in your pocket or otherwise out of sight you end up fumbling around a lot before you get the desired response . Second , iRiver has released a 6GB H10 in the same case and and 20 GB version , which is a bit bulkier , both for under $ 300 . So the only reason to buy the 5GB H10 is to save $ 30 . Finally , 3.7 GB of music on my PC will not fit on this 5 GB player , which only has 4.6 gigs available for music . Somehow the music expands during the synchronization process . I have queried both iRiver and Microsoft by email about this and received no response . PS Be sure to download and install the firmware upgrade before you start .

text: I have done a lot of research on the different options -LRB- Roku , Apple TV , and the boxee box -RRB- and I must say that so far so good . My main concern was being able to stream my personal collection of movies and TV shows over a hard line -LRB- powerline network -RRB- . I have a lot of 720\/1080p that requires a lot of bandwidth . As such , I was playing with the idea of building a home theater PC , but the cost\/benefit is not great . The things that sold me on the boxee box are as follows : \* Local file playback \* File format support \* Ethernet connection \* Cost \* Ease of use I did a lot of looking around and I was stuck between this and the Roku . I decided to go with the boxee box because of its local streaming ability and I have been pretty happy with it thus far . There are a few things that I am not too happy with , which is why I deducted a star . They are as follows : \* No Amazon Prime Compatibility -LRB- this is pretty big -RRB- \* It takes a long time to scan and ID my media library \* It did not recognize a lot of TV shows that I arranged by folder\/season\/episode and named 1 , 2 , 3 , etc. . In all fairness , I can still go to files and browse them manually , but it doesnt have the cover art\/information as when it is identified . That makes it harder for my technologically challenged better half to use . I have started to manually identify files , but that is more difficult as it does not show the folder path so I have to play an episode , figure out what it is , and have it try to ID the rest of the folder . It would be so much easier to be able to see the file path and ID it as such . These complaints are fairly minor and there are workable alternatives . I must admit , the boxee box is the best thing short of a HTPC . The cost and ease of setup\/use make it a much more viable option for me . As of right now , it is the sole thing that is streaming my media and I am pretty happy to have it . I would recommend it for those who are looking for a similar set of requirements .
label: Media Player

text: First off , I did n't even know this product existed until shortly before Christmas of last year , when it came to the attention of my wife and I while we were brainstorming on what might be a reasonable , useful and fun Christmas present . A quick look at the WD community forums will give you a laundry list of firmware update related issues where something that previously worked will stop working with a new firmware update . I read -LRB- but have not personally confirmed -RRB- that a firmware update took away the option to send audio over HDMI and TOS-LINK optical cable at the same time . A feature I used before we moved . The TV was set to use audio from HDMI , but I could use my surround sound gaming headset to watch movies without waking my kids . Some videos will work well with one firmware , but studder or lose audio sync with a newer firmware . As annoying as it was that the firmware was never `` right '' , the latest update is what really frustrated me . The latest update broke the device 's ability to use my 1.5 TB Seagate GoFlex portable HDD . The USB activity light would just blink repeatedly until I turned it off . I called customer support , and during my 20 minutes on hold , I was able to find instructions on how to use a USB thumb drive to load the previous firmware , and spent another 20 minutes with the agent rolling back the firmware . The agent told me that it sounded like a problem with my device , and that they 'd exchange the device for me if I had proof of purchase within the last 12 months . The device has barely been on the market for 12 months . -RRB- My wife bought this for me for Christmas at Costco , which meant she used cash , and we 've moved since then and she no longer has a receipt . I explained that the product had barely hit the market 12 months ago , that it was a gift from my wife , and that we 've moved since she bought it , and that we do not have a receipt , but that it was bought at Christmas time . I also confirmed that their product was broken by their firmware update . The response was that WD ca n't do anything for me without an original receipt . Now , every time I try to use the device , I have to navigate away from the automatic `` Firmware Update Found '' to get to my media content . If you buy this device , be prepared to lose features and have inconsistent functionality as new firmwares are released . The device would be amazing if it both worked on all files and had a faster interface , it 'd be great if it did one of those , it 'd be good if the firmware made things better , but it 's simply bad with inconsistent performance , a slow interface , firmware updates that can break the device and take away features , and poor support from WD .

text: Tired of paying $ 24.95 \/ mo for what was essentially an obsolete e-mail device , I decided to replace my old WebTV plus unit which we had used for 8 years with something more high tech . I got a pocket PC for my wife to use for her e-mail , scheduling , some web browsing -LRB- quick weather check -RRB- , etc. . From the beginning I had problems configuring the pocket PC with our DSL network . Sometimes it connected , and sometimes it did n't . I also could n't get the Outlook interface to work with the e-mail settings . It would receive , but it would n't send . Since my wife is decidedly non-geeky , I was afraid she would give up using it in frustration , and it would become a very expensive paperweight . In the meantime I was selling the WebTV unit and looking for a pdf of the Users Guide when I happened to check out the information on MSN TV 2 . It was a revelation . WebTV had gone broadband ! The more I read the more it seemed like the perfect solution - maintain the familiar comfort level of WebTV for my wife and add new and faster functions using our existing network . I immediately ordered one and that night returned the pocket PC for a refund and picked up the MSN TV 2 unit . Set up was very easy . I used Ethernet cable -LRB- not included -RRB- to connect to the router and the supplied wiring to connect to the TV . It immediately found the network and walked me through setting up and transferring the account from the old WebTV to MSN TV . I chose the discount broadband option -LRB- goodbye $ 25\/mo ! -RRB- . The response time was very quick , the keyboard has a nice feel to it , and we played around with streaming video and audio from the internet . Everything worked as advertised . Later I connected over the network to my laptop PC with no trouble and ran a slideshow of our latest camping weekend trip . I was also able to view video saved on the laptop 's hard drive . The MSN TV 2 network interface was quick , and the file structure mirrored what was on the laptop . All I had to do was share out the files on the laptop to make it happen . My wife picked right up and caught up on the e-mail that had been piling up since I bought the pocket PC . Broadband makes this unit well worth the price and then some . I would n't have picked it if it still only operated using dial-up and did n't offer the networking option . Two small concerns keep me from giving this 5 stars instead of 4 : -LRB- a -RRB- there is an OK key on the keyboard right where I would expect a Scroll Down to be ; I keep opening a new page or link when all I want to do is move down the page . -LRB- b -RRB- Because I set the account up using my old WebTV address , that account ca n't access the Junk Mail or POP mail retrieval options described in the Help section . Tech support -LRB- quick but overseas -RRB- tells me that change is coming , however . For $ 100 -LRB- after the $ 100 rebate offer -RRB- , this is a GREAT DEAL , especially as a second unit in the living room attached to a BIG monitor !
label: Media Player

text: First off , I want to give major kudos to Microsoft for putting out a very solid , iPod competitor . I purchased a Zune on it 's first day out , and I must say that it has some major benefits over the current 5th Generation iPod . Currently , I own both a 30gb iPod Video and the 30gb Zune . I will try and be as unbiased and accurate as possible . This review will focus on the Zune 's strong points vs the Apple 30gb video iPod . Section 1 : External Look and feel I will be the first to admit that when I first saw a picture of the Zune I thought it still had a ways to go , but the more pictures I saw -LRB- and even more once I held saw one in the store -RRB- the more I began to love how unique it is . The semi-transparent , rubberish exterior is a joy to hold . I was torn between Brown and Black , but I ended up with Brown because it had a nice vintage , cool feel to it . The Black is very impressive as well . It is very sleek and the blue double-shot makes it look very modern . Sitting next to my Black iPod it looks better , the only thing that I do n't like is that the circle cover over the 4 way controls is slightly loose -LRB- on mine -RRB- and it makes it feel a little cheaper than the iPod . This thing is rugged and durable , though . Something that I never felt about my iPod . When I first purchased my iPod , I immediately put it into a thick case , while with the Zune it feels like it 's already to go , whether it be in my pocket , backpack or on the seat of my car . I find it funny that people call it a brick as a derogatory nickname , when it feels very durable -LRB- like a brick teehee -RRB- Section 2 : Navigation on the Zune Hands down Zune knocks the iPod out of the park for me . If you have looked around at other players , the Zune uses the Toshiba Gigabeat-style navigation and it 's quite seamless . It 's brilliant . You use your left and right buttons on the center `` wheel '' to Navigate through your Artists , Albums , Playlists , Songs , etc. then you vertically scroll to each song , picture or video . It 's hard to explain , but it is very quick and easy to use . Once you have a song playing , you press the center button to get a range of easy to select options -LRB- 5-Star Rating System , Show song list , Shuffle , Repeat , send , and flag -RRB- . With the iPod you have to pan through these options rather than seeing them all at once . The iPod does not have the quick shuffle and quick repeat options that Zune has readily available on each song you play . The learning curve for using the Zune menus is practically non-existent . Finding songs on the iPod is no less difficult , but these quick options and ease of use are really what sets the Zune apart . One thing I have n't mentioned is how good it looks while you are navigating your content . The subtle yet very noticeable animations when going between songs are very tasteful . That 's not to say that the iPod 's menus are not tasteful as well , but the Zune gives you a little more life that makes it better -LRB- imho -RRB- . All in all you will fly through your tunes on the Zune . I found myself listening to 10 seconds of each song just because I liked jumping through the menus . I know , I 'm a geek ; but hey , they look good ! The only thing that I noticed that was bad about the animations is that the Anti-Aliasing -LRB- type smoothing -RRB- seems to disappear when changing menus . This is barely noticable and is n't a deterrent for me at all . It 's just something that I noticed . Skipping to different parts of Audio and Video is better with the iPod 's scroll wheel . The Zune does well though , it seems when holding down the right button -LRB- fast forward -RRB- it has 4 speeds that it cycles through pending how long you hold it -LRB- the longer you hold the faster it goes -RRB- . With the iPod it 's easier to let your thumb pace the speed that you want to navigate . That 's not to say that the Zune 's buttons are bad . I prefer the Zune buttons over the iPod 's for one reason really . The scroll wheel was just too hard to use while driving . I constantly found myself over scrolling songs . With the Zune , you can easily see that the tack you want is X amount of clicks away . It makes it much easier to keep your eyes on the road . Section 3 : Audio and Video playback Being a musician and somewhat of an audiophile I can say that the Zune and iPod are on pretty level playing fields when it comes to audio playback . I did a quick switch using the Zune 's headphones on both the Zune and my iPod and could n't notice a difference . I went back and forth at least 10 times on a few songs at and I could n't tell a difference . The iPod has more EQ presets than the Zune does , but the Zune 's cover a decent enough range -LRB- not that I would n't mind having more -RRB- . As for video I 'm going to give a little to both competitors . The Zune has bright colors and a very clear screen , but with the pre-loaded videos I saw more grain than I 'm used to coming from the iPod . The iPod screen is pretty much perfect besides the size . Videos and photos are crystal clear . Viewing photos is a more pleasurable experience on the Zune because it seems to be just as clear as the iPod , and the screen is bigger . I 'm not sure why viewing video suffers and viewing photos does n't on the Zune , but with the pre-loaded content that 's the way it looks . I still prefer the bigger screen over a small loss in video quality . Section 4 : Zune Features Wi-Fi and FM Radio I just want to quickly highlight the FM Radio and the Wi-Fi features of the Zune . I 'm about an hour away from the Cities and the Zune 's Radio reception has been poor so far . It seems pretty inconsistent where I am . Almost to the point of not being able to use it . This was pretty disappointing . The wireless has been so dumbed down that it 's practically worthless for me . As of now the Zune does n't have a big fan base . I 'm doing my best to get my friends interested , but as it stands I do n't have anyone to share my tunes with . It 's disappointing to have a feature with such strong potential and not use it to it 's full extent . The Wi-Fi being the thing that 's supposed to separate the Zune from the iPod , takes a huge blow right away because of the uphill battle that Microsoft has to fight to get more users . Section 5 : Zune Software and Wrap-up I 'll be brief -LRB- for once -RRB- the Zune Marketplace is an elegant easy-to-use piece of software . It imported my music seemlessly and is a breeze to find the artists you know and discover ones you do n't . I hope their playlists become a little more robust in the future because as of now they are pretty thin . The player handles music and ratings beautifully . It has a pretty straight-forward interface . I do like iTunes way of displaying how much and what type of data you have on your iPod . I have n't seen anything as nice as that in the Zune Marketplace . In closing , I 'll give you one of my main reasons for getting the Zune in four words . All You Can Eat . I am a person who wants to try out as many different artists as I want with no commitment -LRB- other than $ 15\/mo of course -RRB- . With the Zune Pass , I flew through their labyrinth of music files -LRB- still 1 million less than iTunes -RRB- and found tons of artists to stack my 30gb with . Most people like to own their music and that 's well and good , but if Apple had a subscription service , I may not have purchased the Zune . The Zune is a GREAT player , which I feel is much better than the iPod . If this is your first time buying a mp3 player , get the Zune . It 's the hands down winner . More features , bigger screen , beautiful interface , very stable and has a little more personality than the iPod . If you 're already an iPod owner and do n't need things like wi-fi , fm radio and subscription based music , the Zune probably is n't worth a possibly expensive switch . I 'm lovin ' my Zune , though . I 've been having so much more fun with it than my iPod , but that 's just me . Go over to Target or BestBuy if you can to check it out ! - = Edit = - 11\/17\/06 Section 6 : What the Zune is missing It has been brought to my attention that I missed a few key points . I am not a ` fanboy ' of either Microsoft or Apple . These were mute points for me , but I realize that some people may really want some of these functions . The biggest one is that the iPod can be used as a portable mass storage device while the Zune can not -LRB- currently -RRB- . With the iPod you just plug it in and you have the ability to create folders . The iPod has a whole extras section that 's missing from the Zune . I did n't remember to mention them because I never used them . They are : 1 . Clock I have not found a clock on the Zune . I would n't mind having one , I usually go to my cell phone before I go to my mp3 player to check the time . 2 . Games The games that come on the iPod are pretty basic , but they beat Zune 's wopping 0 games . Personally , I played them about one time . 3 . Contacts You have the ability to add contact information ranging from phone numbers to web addresses and home addresses . I did n't use this feature . 4 . Calendar The iPod has built in Calendar functionality . 5 . Notes This is one that is actually going to be hard to lose . I often would throw directions to new locations right on to my iPod to save me the ` trouble ' of printing . It was a nice convenience that you will have to live without . Notes work by dragging text documents to your iPod notes folder . I never had any problems with it . The text rendering is pretty basic . 6 . Stop Watch The iPod has a nice little stop watch built in . I never used it , but I 'd assume it would be nice for more active people than I. 7 . I may have mentioned this , but the Zune Marketplace has about 1 Million less songs than iTunes and currently does n't have any videos available for download where as iTunes has a plethora of tv shows and movies to choose from . I was always aware of this , but I have no doubt that Microsoft will be expanding it 's library pretty fast . They have a lot of catching up to do . In closing -LRB- again ? -RRB- The Zune is the right player for me because of all of the points that I have mentioned . The iTunes has a lot more of the smaller features that I do n't really appreciate as much in a PMP . Most of the extras on the iPod I do on my phone . But the mass storage thing is a pretty huge hit , and even though I have n't sold my iPod yet I already miss my notes . The Zune has some tweaks that definitely could help it out , but it is a joy to use as is . When it comes to listening to music and ease of use , the Zune is the winner imho . I will give the iPod credit for it 's larger amount of extra features . If I could change my rating of the Zune , it would be a 4 . I hope this helped Cheers
label: Media Player

text: I shopped around a lot before buying this player , and it was difficult for me due to the fact that almost every review is extremely biased . iPod fanatics insist that their favored product is far superior , while Creative fanboys tend to do the same . I , for one , do not think that neither the zen nor the ipod come out as a clear victor , but are simply two good players that will be right for a person depending on what they want to get out of it . The Zen , to me , has advantages . For one , the video out port on the charging adapter is excellent . The picture looks great on my HD , and has essentially replaced my dvd player due to convenience . Furthermore , I 'm enjoying my xvid encoded movies far more easily . Those of you familiar with getting videos on the internet will know how ubiquitous the xvid and divx codecs are , and it turns out to be a very nice feature when combined with the video output of the zen . My experience with ipod video on televisions has not been nearly as enjoyable . The equalizer remains another preferable feature for me to choose the zen . The lack of an equalizer on the ipod puzzles me , as one would imagine an 80 GB player is meant for the market of the more audio-savvy among us , who care enough to change the audio settings of our music . I know this is not a critical factor , but I for one enjoy the ability to customize the background of my player . Its nice knowing that I can just throw on any picture of my whim and utilize it as a wallpaper for my player . The ipod outdoes the zen in terms of storage , size , and the ability to play games . I admit that I would prefer a thinner player , though the thickness is the zen is not so severe that I can not easily slide it in my pocket . The ipod does offer an extra 20 gb on their player , but one must also pay for the extra storage , it is a more pricey player . Games are a nice feature , but as far as I 'm concerned , it is not something to complain about a lack of ; this is a music player . The aesthetics and interfaces of the two players are equally nice as far as I 'm concerned . As far as durability the reviews that I 've read seem to imply that the Zen scratches more , but I 've kept mine naked -LRB- I left the sticker on the screen from when I got it , and leave it in the included pouch when I 'm not using it ... purchased a podskin and an invisishield but decided not to employ either -RRB- and it is fine . People must treat their pmp 's quite brutally , because I have seen nary a scratch on my player in the approximate six months that I 've owned it . My cohorts ' ipods all seem to have taken more wear than my zen , especially on that shiny chrome back . Is the Zen a good player ? Yes . Is the ipod a good player ? Yes . Pick what fits your needs , and you will be most satisfied . I would give this product a 4.5 more accurately , simply knocking off a half point wishing that the Thin Model 60gb zen was in America , which is the same thickness as the 30gb one . Everything else on the player works great .
label: Media Player

text: The hardware is fine . I got this thing for $ 90 when Zune Brown was going for a song . And now I almost think I should send it back . $ 90 for a 30GB hard drive is a great deal ... or so I thought . But you really ca n't use it like a hard drive , and it 's all down hill from there . You have to use -- you can ONLY use -- the software that comes with it , which is n't merely bad . It 's evil . I write software for a living . I thought , `` How bad can it be ? '' Well , at least as far as I know , it has n't messed up anything other than itself . And that 's about the best thing I can say for it . Everything was going fine til yesterday , when , and you 're not going to believe I was STUPID enough to do this ... I plugged the Zune into my computer to charge it . Then , last night , I went to listen to some music , and IT WAS ALL GONE ! Every single track came up as `` Item is missing or can not be played . '' Every single track . So , I searched the web . Microsoft 's knowledge base basically said , `` Start over '' and went on to list all the different levels of `` starting over , '' the first and least severe of which is removing all the music from the Zune and re-synching . So , I did that . And I thought all was well . NOT ! I went to put a few new CD 's I 'd ripped on the Zune . I synched them up , went to play them , and again , EVERY SINGLE TRACK WAS GONE !!! So ... I started over again , except now I noticed that all those old copies out there were still taking up space on the Zune , and now it was nearly full , even though I 've only got about 10MB of content . So , now I 've erased ALL the content from the Zune -- the music , the pictures , the video , and I 'm re-synching again . We 'll see ...
